Green Electricity from Biomass, Part II: Environmental Impacts Considering Avoided Burdens from Replacing the Conventional Provision of Additional Functions
Article in Waste and Biomass Valorization March 2012 DOI:10.1007/s12649-011-9091-5
Sonja Siegl Forschungsinstitut für biologischen Landbau 6 PUBLICATIONS 77 CITATIONS
Peter Holubar University of Natural Resources and Life Sciences Vienna 33 PUBLICATIONS 779 CITATIONS
<title>Green Electricity from Biomass, Part II: Environmental Impacts Considering Avoided Burdens from Replacing the Conventional Provision of Additional Functions </title>
Sonja Siegl . Michael Laaber Peter Holubar
Received: 18 March 2011 / Accepted: 3 September 2011 /Published online: 20 September 2011 © Springer Science+Business Media B.V. 2011

Abstract Environmental impacts of the introduction of electricity generation from biomass are investigated on the basis of data from plants operated in Austria. Different conversion technologies (anaerobic digestion, combustion, gasification) are considered and environmental impacts are analyzed according to ISO 14040 for Life Cycle Assess- ment. For the impact assessment the CML 2001 base line approach covering 11 impact categories is used. The paper presents the calculation of the avoided environmental burdens from replacing the conventional provision of functions by the additional functions of the biomass sys- tem. These additional functions are the provision of heat, nutrients and waste disposal. The conventional provision of heat and the disposal of manure and organic residues show the highest influence on total results. After the consider- ation of avoided burdens emission-savings are calculated in the impact categories GWP, ADP, ODP, AP and EP, while emissions remain in the impact categories POCP, LUC, HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP for most of the plant types. In comparison with the UCTE-electricity-produc- tion-mix total emissions are lower for almost all plant types and impact categories. A ranking of the total results based on the unweighed aggregation of all impact category results is lead by anaerobic digestion of mainly organic residues followed by gasification of wood chips, the anaerobic digestion in small plants and combustion of
wood chips. These results show that it is essential to con- sider both direct and indirect environmental impacts.
Keywords Life cycle assessment . Green electricity Anaerobic digestion . Combustion . Gasification . Biomass

5: At the moment the development of different renewable energy technologies and an increase of the share of "re- newables" on the total energy production is the target of European as well as international agreements dealing with energy and climate change. The potential environmental benefits that can be obtained from an increased use of bioenergy derived from renewable biomass sources are the main driving forces for the promotion of its production and use. Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is considered as one of the best methodologies for the evaluation of the environ- mental impacts associated with bioenergy production. It identifies energy and materials used, as well as waste and emissions released to the environment [1].

The most common conversion technologies used for electricity generation from biomass in Austria are anaero- bic digestion of energy crops, manure and organic resi- dues and combustion (and gasification) of wood chips. LCAs dealing with bioenergy have been conducted by Börjesson and Berglund [2] or Spielmann et al. [3] for anaerobic digestion, by Jungmeier et al. [4] or Bauer et al. [5] for combustion of wood chips and by Pucker et al. [6] for gasification. LCA results of electricity generation from biomass depend largely on methodological assumptions like the choice of functional unit, allocation method or consideration of land use changes, which makes the com- parison of results from different studies difficult. To be able to present the results of the life cycle assessment of green electricity from biomass in a transparent and traceable manner they are divided into two parts: Part I [7] covers direct life cycle emissions, while the consideration of additional functions and allocation questions is handled in Part II.

The present paper therefore, aims at providing data on the environmental impacts of the introduction of electricity generation in small scale biomass plants with consideration of avoided burdens through the replacement of the con- ventional provision of additional functions.

1 The term "residues" is defined in this paper as organic wastes produced during the production process of other products which are not part of the system under investigation. As given in [7] the organic residues used are draff, glycerine from RME production, kitchen leftovers, lawn cutting, oil seed residues, potato residues, sugar beet cuttings, vegetable residues or wheat mill residues. In practice they have no economic value or producers even have to pay to be allowed to dispose them.

For the evaluation of the environmental effects emis- sions in 11 impact categories (global warming potential (GWP), acidification potential (AP), eutrophication poten- tial (EP), photochemical oxidants creation potential (POCP), abiotic depletion potential (ADP), ozone depletion potential (ODP), land use (LUC) and different toxicity potentials (HTP, TAETP, FAETP, MAETP)) are calculated for different reference systems. The results are used to compare the different conversion technologies, to identify crucial life cycle stages and to reveal potentials for improvement.

8: <header>Allocation in LCA of Bioenergy Systems </header>

The process of assigning to each of the functions of a multiple-function system only those environmental burdens associated with that particular function is called allocation [8]. Allocation of environmental burdens is one of the continuing methodological problems in LCA. It arises in multiple-input (waste treatment processes), multiple-output (co-product systems), and multiple-use systems (open-loop recycling) [9].

Biomass systems are multiple-function systems: starting from substrates which are wastes of other production sys- tems (function of manure and organic residues disposal) they produce various amounts of additional co-products (e.g. heat, nutrients) in addition to the main product, electricity.

The ISO standards [10] propose that allocation should be avoided if possible either through the division of the whole process into sub-processes related to co-products or by expanding the system boundaries to include the addi- tional functions related to the co-products (called "system expansion approach" or "substitution approach").

If allocation cannot be avoided the environmental bur- dens can be partitioned according to a physical relationship like the mass-, energy- or exergy content or according to other relationships including the economic value.

The subdivision approach rarely avoids allocation completely because most multiple-function systems include processes which are common for some or all of its functional outputs SO that some kind of allocation will still be necessary [9, 11].
Regarding the substitution approach, there are two possible ways of applying the system expansion. For both options the following systems have to be defined [8]:

<list>- "System I, producing a functional output of interest together with other co-products in the system, 
- System II, producing only the functional output of interest, and 
- System III, producing all co-products from System I except the functional output of interest."</list>

The direct system enlargement compares System I and Systems II+III. The avoided burdens approach, subtracts from System I the environmental burdens from System III. Thus System I-III is compared with System II.

According to literature sources dealing with allocation in general [8, 9, 11] the avoided burdens approach is useful in cases where output of co-products from one system displaces their production elsewhere, as in recovery of energy from waste or in co-generation of electricity and thermal energy.

Especially in the field of biofuels the impact of choosing different allocation approaches has been studied in various publications [12-15]. They identified the following limi- tations of the substitution approach:

<list>- the possibility that several potential replacements/ substitutes exist and that it is not possible to define the most realistic alternative product, 
- the significant dependence of the results on the substitute which is chosen for the reference system, 
- the need of highly accurate data on the alternative product and difficulties with their availability, 
- the possibility that the market of the most realistic substitute may be restricted.</list>
The first two limitations can be overcome by conducting a detailed sensitivity analysis using different alternative substitutes.
Cherubini et al. [1] do not recommend the system expansion approach when a large number of high-quality outputs/co-products is produced. But for heat and nutrients (digestate) this is not the case.
Ekvall and Finnveden [11] conclude that "the system expansion approach is a possible and adequate way of dealing with allocation if

<list>- data can be obtained for the substitute, 
- data uncertainties are not too large, and 
- indirect effects are important enough to be significant for a decision."</list>

Several studies concerning LCA of bioenergy [6, 16-18] use the system expansion or substitution approach to han- dle the allocation problem. A review paper on LCA of bioenergy systems [19] also identified the substitution approach as the most commonly used allocation method.
Land use in LCA of Bioenergy Systems

According to Cherubini et al. [1] biomass for bioenergy production can be obtained by changing the use of existing biomass (bioenergy production instead of food/feed), crop area expansion, shortening of the rotation length, and yield increment on the same area. With the introduction of bioenergy systems a SO called "land use change" occurs, because the cultivation of

biomass for bioenergy production requires an agricultural plot that was previously used, and would otherwise be used, for a different purpose (e.g. food/feed production, set aside land, forest etc.) [20]. Land use change due to bio- energy production can occur in two ways, (1) directly, when uncultivated land, pasture etc. is converted to pro- duce energy crops or (2) indirectly, through displacement of food/feed crop production to new land areas previously not used for cultivation [20-22]. Therefore, the alternative land use and/or alternative biomass use should be consid- ered [22] ("Reference use land" in this study).

Cherubini et al. [1] do not recommend the system expansion approach when a large number of high-quality outputs/co-products is produced. But for heat and nutrients (digestate) this is not the case.
Ekvall and Finnveden [11] conclude that "the system expansion approach is a possible and adequate way of dealing with allocation if

<list>- data can be obtained for the substitute, 
- data uncertainties are not too large, and 
- indirect effects are important enough to be significant for a decision."</list>

Several studies concerning LCA of bioenergy [6, 16-18] use the system expansion or substitution approach to han- dle the allocation problem. A review paper on LCA of bioenergy systems [19] also identified the substitution approach as the most commonly used allocation method.
Land use in LCA of Bioenergy Systems

According to Cherubini et al. [1] biomass for bioenergy production can be obtained by changing the use of existing biomass (bioenergy production instead of food/feed), crop area expansion, shortening of the rotation length, and yield increment on the same area. With the introduction of bioenergy systems a SO called "land use change" occurs, because the cultivation of

biomass for bioenergy production requires an agricultural plot that was previously used, and would otherwise be used, for a different purpose (e.g. food/feed production, set aside land, forest etc.) [20]. Land use change due to bio- energy production can occur in two ways, (1) directly, when uncultivated land, pasture etc. is converted to pro- duce energy crops or (2) indirectly, through displacement of food/feed crop production to new land areas previously not used for cultivation [20-22]. Therefore, the alternative land use and/or alternative biomass use should be consid- ered [22] ("Reference use land" in this study).
12: <header>Allocation in LCA of Bioenergy Systems </header>

The process of assigning to each of the functions of a multiple-function system only those environmental burdens associated with that particular function is called allocation [8]. Allocation of environmental burdens is one of the continuing methodological problems in LCA. It arises in multiple-input (waste treatment processes), multiple-output (co-product systems), and multiple-use systems (open-loop recycling) [9].

Biomass systems are multiple-function systems: starting from substrates which are wastes of other production sys- tems (function of manure and organic residues disposal) they produce various amounts of additional co-products (e.g. heat, nutrients) in addition to the main product, electricity.

The ISO standards [10] propose that allocation should be avoided if possible either through the division of the whole process into sub-processes related to co-products or by expanding the system boundaries to include the addi- tional functions related to the co-products (called "system expansion approach" or "substitution approach").

If allocation cannot be avoided the environmental bur- dens can be partitioned according to a physical relationship like the mass-, energy- or exergy content or according to other relationships including the economic value.

The subdivision approach rarely avoids allocation completely because most multiple-function systems include processes which are common for some or all of its functional outputs SO that some kind of allocation will still be necessary [9, 11].
Regarding the substitution approach, there are two possible ways of applying the system expansion. For both options the following systems have to be defined [8]:

<list>- "System I, producing a functional output of interest together with other co-products in the system, 
- System II, producing only the functional output of interest, and 
- System III, producing all co-products from System I except the functional output of interest."</list>

The direct system enlargement compares System I and Systems II+III. The avoided burdens approach, subtracts from System I the environmental burdens from System III. Thus System I-III is compared with System II.

According to literature sources dealing with allocation in general [8, 9, 11] the avoided burdens approach is useful in cases where output of co-products from one system displaces their production elsewhere, as in recovery of energy from waste or in co-generation of electricity and thermal energy.

Especially in the field of biofuels the impact of choosing different allocation approaches has been studied in various publications [12-15]. They identified the following limi- tations of the substitution approach:

<list>- the possibility that several potential replacements/ substitutes exist and that it is not possible to define the most realistic alternative product, 
- the significant dependence of the results on the substitute which is chosen for the reference system, 
- the need of highly accurate data on the alternative product and difficulties with their availability, 
- the possibility that the market of the most realistic substitute may be restricted.</list>
The first two limitations can be overcome by conducting a detailed sensitivity analysis using different alternative substitutes.
Cherubini et al. [1] do not recommend the system expansion approach when a large number of high-quality outputs/co-products is produced. But for heat and nutrients (digestate) this is not the case.
Ekvall and Finnveden [11] conclude that "the system expansion approach is a possible and adequate way of dealing with allocation if

<list>- data can be obtained for the substitute, 
- data uncertainties are not too large, and 
- indirect effects are important enough to be significant for a decision."</list>

Several studies concerning LCA of bioenergy [6, 16-18] use the system expansion or substitution approach to han- dle the allocation problem. A review paper on LCA of bioenergy systems [19] also identified the substitution approach as the most commonly used allocation method.
Land use in LCA of Bioenergy Systems

According to Cherubini et al. [1] biomass for bioenergy production can be obtained by changing the use of existing biomass (bioenergy production instead of food/feed), crop area expansion, shortening of the rotation length, and yield increment on the same area. With the introduction of bioenergy systems a SO called "land use change" occurs, because the cultivation of

biomass for bioenergy production requires an agricultural plot that was previously used, and would otherwise be used, for a different purpose (e.g. food/feed production, set aside land, forest etc.) [20]. Land use change due to bio- energy production can occur in two ways, (1) directly, when uncultivated land, pasture etc. is converted to pro- duce energy crops or (2) indirectly, through displacement of food/feed crop production to new land areas previously not used for cultivation [20-22]. Therefore, the alternative land use and/or alternative biomass use should be consid- ered [22] ("Reference use land" in this study).
13: The environmental impacts are determined according to the international standard ISO 14040 (2006) for Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) [10].
Goal of the study is to assess the environmental impacts of the introduction of small scale biomass plants for elec- tricity generation and to compare different conversion technologies.

Therefore, not only direct life cycle emissions resulting from biomass production, transport, the operation of the biomass plant, the disposal or utilization of the co-products and the construction/demolition of the plant have to be considered, but also indirect emissions. The study focuses especially on the environmental burdens which will be avoided by the introduction of the small scale biomass plants because they replace the conventional production of co-products [e.g. heat, nutrients (digestate)] or the con- ventional provision of additional functions (disposal of manure and organic residues). The biomass conversion technologies covered by the study are anaerobic digestion of different compositions of energy crops, manure and organic residues in different plant sizes with different CHP-engines as well as combustion and gasification of wood-chips (see Table 1). A more detailed description of the technologies is given in [7]. Functional unit is 1 kWh electricity generated at the plant.
14: The environmental impacts are determined according to the international standard ISO 14040 (2006) for Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) [10].
Goal of the study is to assess the environmental impacts of the introduction of small scale biomass plants for elec- tricity generation and to compare different conversion technologies.

Therefore, not only direct life cycle emissions resulting from biomass production, transport, the operation of the biomass plant, the disposal or utilization of the co-products and the construction/demolition of the plant have to be considered, but also indirect emissions. The study focuses especially on the environmental burdens which will be avoided by the introduction of the small scale biomass plants because they replace the conventional production of co-products [e.g. heat, nutrients (digestate)] or the con- ventional provision of additional functions (disposal of manure and organic residues). The biomass conversion technologies covered by the study are anaerobic digestion of different compositions of energy crops, manure and organic residues in different plant sizes with different CHP-engines as well as combustion and gasification of wood-chips (see Table 1). A more detailed description of the technologies is given in [7]. Functional unit is 1 kWh electricity generated at the plant.

<list>- data for the possible substitutes are obtained from plant operators; 
- data uncertainties are not higher than for direct life cycle emissions and 
- indirect effects showed to be very important for the overall results.</list>
Where different possible substitutes were identified the effects on the total results were determined in a sensitivity analysis.

For the co-product heat for example the following assumption is made: if a usable quantity of heat produced as a co-product is used in such a way that it avoids the production of heat by more conventional means (= sub- stitute or reference system), the environmental load of the avoided, conventional heat production was subtracted from the emissions of the biomass system (= credit).

In the present study the functional output of interest is electricity. Electricity production is the main reason for the construction of the biomass plants under investigation. Additional functions provided by the different biomass systems are given in Table 2. The plants would not explicitly be built in order to provide these additional functions. Nev- ertheless they substitute processes which would otherwise be necessary to provide the additional functions if the plants did not exist (e.g. heat supply by conventional fossil fuels).

In this study the term "reference system" refers to all the processes needed for the substitution of the additional functions (e.g. conventional heat production or storage and application of undigested manure). The assumed reference systems are also given in Table 2.
As stated in the introduction, a practical problem of the avoided burdens approach is the selection of the substitutes

[4, 12-15, 23]. Information on the conventional disposal/ management of manure and organic residues used as sub- strates in the biomass-plants was obtained in personal interviews with plant operators.

For the co-product heat a fossil heat supply mix according to a study by the BMU, Germany [24] was assumed as the substitute (see "reference production heat"). According to this study, this heat supply mix rep- resents the additional fossil-based energy sources that would have to be used for heat generation if the biomass- based heat generation capacities were unavailable.
For the co-product nutrients according to the common agricultural practice conventional, synthetic N-fertilizer is assumed to be the substitute.
System Boundary

According to the ISO standards [10] the system boundary "defines the unit processes to be included in the system to be modeled". Figure 1 describes the life cycle stages considered within the system boundary. The unit processes of each life cycle stage are described in the Life Cycle Inventory, where it is also stated for which unit processes regional data has been collected and for which unit pro- cesses data from a database have been used.

Life cycle stages which cover direct life cycle emissions of electricity generation in biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of the study [7] and are shown in light collared boxes in Fig. 1. To cover indirect environmental impacts or avoided burdens from the replacement of the conventional production or provision of the additional functions the following life cycle stages have been intro- duced: "reference use land", "reference use manure",

Life Cycle Inventory

Regional data of 34 biomass plants all over Austria were collected in personal interviews with plant operators for anaerobic digestion plants by Laaber et al. [25] and for wood-chip-CHP plants by Siegl [26]. The questionnaire was elaborated in several meetings of plant operators, plant suppliers, planners and bioenergy research representatives. It covered specific process characterizing parameters in three fields:

<list>general functional description (e.g. quality and quantity of substrates, yields, transport distances and means of transportation, substrate dosage, plant type, digestate storage type (covered/open) digestate treatment and application technique, mode of ash disposal, product gas quantity and quality, energy conversion, thermal energy use, etc.) 
measurable process conditions [e.g. COD, TS, VSS, TKN, NH4-N of substrates and digestate, CH4, CO2, H2S, O2-concentration in biogas, flue gas emissions (CO, CH4, NMVOC, NOx, particulates, SO2), etc.] 
calculable variables (e.g. substrate input in t/year, especially for AD: residence time, hydraulic load, VSS degradation, digestate production in t/year, emissions</list>
from digestate, biogas/methane yield, calorific value, etc.).

For necessary additional calculations of emissions, emission factors and calculation methods are taken from literature, which are described in the following paragraphs. Global data on environmental impacts of transport services or the production of construction materials are taken from the Ecoinvent database [27], see Fig. 1.
Direct Life Cycle Emissions
Data collection, estimations and calculation methods for the calculation of direct life cycle emissions of the different types of biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of this study [7].
Reference Use Land (REF Land)

The need for the incorporation of land use change, caused by biomass production for bioenergy, into the LCA is demonstrated by different literature sources [1, 20-22] and is mentioned in the introduction. According to Bergsma et al. [16] the cultivation of biomass for energy purposes leads to the displacement of crop production as well as the displacement of land cover prior to biomass production for energy.

Data on the alternative land and biomass use (before the introduction of the biomass-systems) have been collected in personal interviews with plant operators.

As a result of the information gathered in the interviews and case studies from literature [6, 16, 21, 28] the reference uses given in Table 3 are assumed for the different types of biomass considered within this study. The assumed refer- ence use for the alternative land use is credited to the biomass system, the assumed reference use of the alter- native biomass use is charged to the biomass system. For reasons of simplicity and better presentability of the results both reference uses for land are combined to one life cycle stage, which can be positive if the effect of the alternative biomass use prevails or negative, if the effect of alternative land use prevails.

Changes in organic carbon stocks due to land use change are especially important for green house gas balances [19,21, 22]. Depending on the earlier use of the land and the crop to be established, the land use change can be a benefit or a disad- vantage depending for example on whether a forest is con- verted to agricultural land for bioenergy production (loss of carbon stocks) or set-aside land is taken into production (carbon stock may increase) [22].

The land use changes assumed for the biomass pro- duction in this study (Table 3) are: set aside land-crop land, crop land-crop land and forest-forest. As most set- aside land in Austria is grassland that is regularly mowed or ploughed [28] and on the other hand 20% of the land that is needed for biomass production for the AD-plants investigated is used for the production of grass-silage it is assumed that no change in organic carbon stocks occurs. If the type of land does not change with the land use change again no change in the organic carbon stocks is assumed. Furthermore because changes of carbon in soil are very site-specific and highly dependent on former and current agronomic practices, climate, and soil characteristics [19]
and there are often uncertainties in the levels of carbon stock changes due to variations in local conditions and lack of reliable field trial data [21].
Reference Use Manure (REF Manure)

By the introduction of anaerobic digestion plants, which use manure as a substrate, the emissions caused by the conventional management of manure (reference use) can be avoided. Therefore, a credit for the "reference use manure" is given to the biomass conversion system.

Data on the yearly amount and type of manure used in AD plants, as well as a possible processing, storage and spreading conditions before the introduction of the AD plant are collected in interviews with plant operators. From these amounts the energy input and emissions caused by the transport to the field and the spreading process (without gaseous emissions originating from the manure itself) are calculated using the corresponding ecoinvent datasets [27].

According to Amon et al. [30] manure management, consisting of the storage and spreading of manure, is a significant source of CH4, NH3 and N2O emissions to the atmosphere. Due to a lack of measurement data, gaseous emissions from manure management are calculated via emission factors derived from different literature sources (see Table 4).

Ammonia and dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the storage occur only from the surface of stored liquid manure [31]. Ammonia emissions are dependent on physical properties like the concentration of total ammoniacal Nitrogen (TAN), dry matter or pH-value as well as envi- ronmental conditions like temperature, wind speed or precipitations [30, 32]. Dinitrogen monoxide is formed under anoxic conditions in the crust (often formed on the surface of the stored manure) when ammonium is nitrified or during the denitrification of nitrate under anaerobic

conditions [33]. For the life cycle inventory they are cal- culated depending on the manure type (cattle or swine) and storage duration in proportion to the surface area of the open storage facilities. Methane emissions from the storage are calculated dependent on the amount of manure stored and the storage duration with different emission factors for cattle and swine manure. Methane emissions from the spreading of manure are very small in comparison to emissions from storage [30]. For the life cycle inventory they are calculated dependent on the amount of manure spread. According to Sommer and Hutchings [31] gaseous emissions from agriculture, and especially from the spreading of solid and liquid manure, are one of the major sources for atmospheric ammonia in Europe. Ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are influenced by a multitude of parameters like concentration of TAN, air temperature, pH-value, evaporation rate, crop canopy size and complexity, crust formation, surface roughness of field, wind speed, solar radiation, total area of manure applied, application technique and infiltration to soil [31]. For the

life cycle inventory ammonia emissions are calculated dependent on the type of manure in proportion to the concentration of TAN. The TAN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. As mentioned above, ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are essentially dependent on the application technique used [31, 33, 36, 53]. If the application technique "trail hose" is used instead of the "splash plate method" ammonia emissions are assumed to be reduced by 40% [31, 33, 36, 53, 54] and for the application technique "injection (closed slot)" by 86% [33, 36, 45, 51, 53, 54].

Dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the spreading of manure are again influenced by many parameters like manuring method, soil characteristics (humidity, concen- tration of Nitrogen or humus) or climatic conditions [33]. For the calculation of the life cycle inventory the IPCC GPG [52] emission factor of 0.0196 (+0.0154) kg N2O (kg TKN) is used. The TKN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. Accord- ing to [36, 45, 51] the closed slot injection application technique increases N2O emissions by a factor of 2.95 in comparison to the "splash plate method".

For reasons of simplicity and better presentability of the results both reference uses for land are combined to one life cycle stage, which can be positive if the effect of the alternative biomass use prevails or negative, if the effect of alternative land use prevails.

Changes in organic carbon stocks due to land use change are especially important for green house gas balances [19,21, 22]. Depending on the earlier use of the land and the crop to be established, the land use change can be a benefit or a disad- vantage depending for example on whether a forest is con- verted to agricultural land for bioenergy production (loss of carbon stocks) or set-aside land is taken into production (carbon stock may increase) [22].

The land use changes assumed for the biomass pro- duction in this study (Table 3) are: set aside land-crop land, crop land-crop land and forest-forest. As most set- aside land in Austria is grassland that is regularly mowed or ploughed [28] and on the other hand 20% of the land that is needed for biomass production for the AD-plants investigated is used for the production of grass-silage it is assumed that no change in organic carbon stocks occurs. If the type of land does not change with the land use change again no change in the organic carbon stocks is assumed. Furthermore because changes of carbon in soil are very site-specific and highly dependent on former and current agronomic practices, climate, and soil characteristics [19]
and there are often uncertainties in the levels of carbon stock changes due to variations in local conditions and lack of reliable field trial data [21].
Reference Use Manure (REF Manure)

By the introduction of anaerobic digestion plants, which use manure as a substrate, the emissions caused by the conventional management of manure (reference use) can be avoided. Therefore, a credit for the "reference use manure" is given to the biomass conversion system.

Data on the yearly amount and type of manure used in AD plants, as well as a possible processing, storage and spreading conditions before the introduction of the AD plant are collected in interviews with plant operators. From these amounts the energy input and emissions caused by the transport to the field and the spreading process (without gaseous emissions originating from the manure itself) are calculated using the corresponding ecoinvent datasets [27].

According to Amon et al. [30] manure management, consisting of the storage and spreading of manure, is a significant source of CH4, NH3 and N2O emissions to the atmosphere. Due to a lack of measurement data, gaseous emissions from manure management are calculated via emission factors derived from different literature sources (see Table 4).

Ammonia and dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the storage occur only from the surface of stored liquid manure [31]. Ammonia emissions are dependent on physical properties like the concentration of total ammoniacal Nitrogen (TAN), dry matter or pH-value as well as envi- ronmental conditions like temperature, wind speed or precipitations [30, 32]. Dinitrogen monoxide is formed under anoxic conditions in the crust (often formed on the surface of the stored manure) when ammonium is nitrified or during the denitrification of nitrate under anaerobic

conditions [33]. For the life cycle inventory they are cal- culated depending on the manure type (cattle or swine) and storage duration in proportion to the surface area of the open storage facilities. Methane emissions from the storage are calculated dependent on the amount of manure stored and the storage duration with different emission factors for cattle and swine manure. Methane emissions from the spreading of manure are very small in comparison to emissions from storage [30]. For the life cycle inventory they are calculated dependent on the amount of manure spread. According to Sommer and Hutchings [31] gaseous emissions from agriculture, and especially from the spreading of solid and liquid manure, are one of the major sources for atmospheric ammonia in Europe. Ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are influenced by a multitude of parameters like concentration of TAN, air temperature, pH-value, evaporation rate, crop canopy size and complexity, crust formation, surface roughness of field, wind speed, solar radiation, total area of manure applied, application technique and infiltration to soil [31]. For the

life cycle inventory ammonia emissions are calculated dependent on the type of manure in proportion to the concentration of TAN. The TAN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. As mentioned above, ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are essentially dependent on the application technique used [31, 33, 36, 53]. If the application technique "trail hose" is used instead of the "splash plate method" ammonia emissions are assumed to be reduced by 40% [31, 33, 36, 53, 54] and for the application technique "injection (closed slot)" by 86% [33, 36, 45, 51, 53, 54].

Dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the spreading of manure are again influenced by many parameters like manuring method, soil characteristics (humidity, concen- tration of Nitrogen or humus) or climatic conditions [33]. For the calculation of the life cycle inventory the IPCC GPG [52] emission factor of 0.0196 (+0.0154) kg N2O (kg TKN) is used. The TKN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. Accord- ing to [36, 45, 51] the closed slot injection application technique increases N2O emissions by a factor of 2.95 in comparison to the "splash plate method".

In the present study the avoided burdens approach has been adopted and the conditions for the utilization of this approach listed in the introduction are met as follows:

<list>- data for the possible substitutes are obtained from plant operators; 
- data uncertainties are not higher than for direct life cycle emissions and 
- indirect effects showed to be very important for the overall results.</list>
Where different possible substitutes were identified the effects on the total results were determined in a sensitivity analysis.

For the co-product heat for example the following assumption is made: if a usable quantity of heat produced as a co-product is used in such a way that it avoids the production of heat by more conventional means (= sub- stitute or reference system), the environmental load of the avoided, conventional heat production was subtracted from the emissions of the biomass system (= credit).

In the present study the functional output of interest is electricity. Electricity production is the main reason for the construction of the biomass plants under investigation. Additional functions provided by the different biomass systems are given in Table 2. The plants would not explicitly be built in order to provide these additional functions. Nev- ertheless they substitute processes which would otherwise be necessary to provide the additional functions if the plants did not exist (e.g. heat supply by conventional fossil fuels).

In this study the term "reference system" refers to all the processes needed for the substitution of the additional functions (e.g. conventional heat production or storage and application of undigested manure). The assumed reference systems are also given in Table 2.
As stated in the introduction, a practical problem of the avoided burdens approach is the selection of the substitutes

[4, 12-15, 23]. Information on the conventional disposal/ management of manure and organic residues used as sub- strates in the biomass-plants was obtained in personal interviews with plant operators.

For the co-product heat a fossil heat supply mix according to a study by the BMU, Germany [24] was assumed as the substitute (see "reference production heat"). According to this study, this heat supply mix rep- resents the additional fossil-based energy sources that would have to be used for heat generation if the biomass- based heat generation capacities were unavailable.
For the co-product nutrients according to the common agricultural practice conventional, synthetic N-fertilizer is assumed to be the substitute.
System Boundary

According to the ISO standards [10] the system boundary "defines the unit processes to be included in the system to be modeled". Figure 1 describes the life cycle stages considered within the system boundary. The unit processes of each life cycle stage are described in the Life Cycle Inventory, where it is also stated for which unit processes regional data has been collected and for which unit pro- cesses data from a database have been used.

Life cycle stages which cover direct life cycle emissions of electricity generation in biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of the study [7] and are shown in light collared boxes in Fig. 1. To cover indirect environmental impacts or avoided burdens from the replacement of the conventional production or provision of the additional functions the following life cycle stages have been intro- duced: "reference use land", "reference use manure",

Life Cycle Inventory

Regional data of 34 biomass plants all over Austria were collected in personal interviews with plant operators for anaerobic digestion plants by Laaber et al. [25] and for wood-chip-CHP plants by Siegl [26]. The questionnaire was elaborated in several meetings of plant operators, plant suppliers, planners and bioenergy research representatives. It covered specific process characterizing parameters in three fields:

<list>general functional description (e.g. quality and quantity of substrates, yields, transport distances and means of transportation, substrate dosage, plant type, digestate storage type (covered/open) digestate treatment and application technique, mode of ash disposal, product gas quantity and quality, energy conversion, thermal energy use, etc.) 
measurable process conditions [e.g. COD, TS, VSS, TKN, NH4-N of substrates and digestate, CH4, CO2, H2S, O2-concentration in biogas, flue gas emissions (CO, CH4, NMVOC, NOx, particulates, SO2), etc.] 
calculable variables (e.g. substrate input in t/year, especially for AD: residence time, hydraulic load, VSS degradation, digestate production in t/year, emissions</list>
from digestate, biogas/methane yield, calorific value, etc.).

For necessary additional calculations of emissions, emission factors and calculation methods are taken from literature, which are described in the following paragraphs. Global data on environmental impacts of transport services or the production of construction materials are taken from the Ecoinvent database [27], see Fig. 1.
Direct Life Cycle Emissions
Data collection, estimations and calculation methods for the calculation of direct life cycle emissions of the different types of biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of this study [7].
Reference Use Land (REF Land)

The need for the incorporation of land use change, caused by biomass production for bioenergy, into the LCA is demonstrated by different literature sources [1, 20-22] and is mentioned in the introduction. According to Bergsma et al. [16] the cultivation of biomass for energy purposes leads to the displacement of crop production as well as the displacement of land cover prior to biomass production for energy.

Data on the alternative land and biomass use (before the introduction of the biomass-systems) have been collected in personal interviews with plant operators.

As a result of the information gathered in the interviews and case studies from literature [6, 16, 21, 28] the reference uses given in Table 3 are assumed for the different types of biomass considered within this study. The assumed refer- ence use for the alternative land use is credited to the biomass system, the assumed reference use of the alter- native biomass use is charged to the biomass system. For reasons of simplicity and better presentability of the results both reference uses for land are combined to one life cycle stage, which can be positive if the effect of the alternative biomass use prevails or negative, if the effect of alternative land use prevails.

Changes in organic carbon stocks due to land use change are especially important for green house gas balances [19,21, 22]. Depending on the earlier use of the land and the crop to be established, the land use change can be a benefit or a disad- vantage depending for example on whether a forest is con- verted to agricultural land for bioenergy production (loss of carbon stocks) or set-aside land is taken into production (carbon stock may increase) [22].

The land use changes assumed for the biomass pro- duction in this study (Table 3) are: set aside land-crop land, crop land-crop land and forest-forest. As most set- aside land in Austria is grassland that is regularly mowed or ploughed [28] and on the other hand 20% of the land that is needed for biomass production for the AD-plants investigated is used for the production of grass-silage it is assumed that no change in organic carbon stocks occurs. If the type of land does not change with the land use change again no change in the organic carbon stocks is assumed. Furthermore because changes of carbon in soil are very site-specific and highly dependent on former and current agronomic practices, climate, and soil characteristics [19]
and there are often uncertainties in the levels of carbon stock changes due to variations in local conditions and lack of reliable field trial data [21].
Reference Use Manure (REF Manure)

By the introduction of anaerobic digestion plants, which use manure as a substrate, the emissions caused by the conventional management of manure (reference use) can be avoided. Therefore, a credit for the "reference use manure" is given to the biomass conversion system.

Data on the yearly amount and type of manure used in AD plants, as well as a possible processing, storage and spreading conditions before the introduction of the AD plant are collected in interviews with plant operators. From these amounts the energy input and emissions caused by the transport to the field and the spreading process (without gaseous emissions originating from the manure itself) are calculated using the corresponding ecoinvent datasets [27].

According to Amon et al. [30] manure management, consisting of the storage and spreading of manure, is a significant source of CH4, NH3 and N2O emissions to the atmosphere. Due to a lack of measurement data, gaseous emissions from manure management are calculated via emission factors derived from different literature sources (see Table 4).

Ammonia and dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the storage occur only from the surface of stored liquid manure [31]. Ammonia emissions are dependent on physical properties like the concentration of total ammoniacal Nitrogen (TAN), dry matter or pH-value as well as envi- ronmental conditions like temperature, wind speed or precipitations [30, 32]. Dinitrogen monoxide is formed under anoxic conditions in the crust (often formed on the surface of the stored manure) when ammonium is nitrified or during the denitrification of nitrate under anaerobic

conditions [33]. For the life cycle inventory they are cal- culated depending on the manure type (cattle or swine) and storage duration in proportion to the surface area of the open storage facilities. Methane emissions from the storage are calculated dependent on the amount of manure stored and the storage duration with different emission factors for cattle and swine manure. Methane emissions from the spreading of manure are very small in comparison to emissions from storage [30]. For the life cycle inventory they are calculated dependent on the amount of manure spread. According to Sommer and Hutchings [31] gaseous emissions from agriculture, and especially from the spreading of solid and liquid manure, are one of the major sources for atmospheric ammonia in Europe. Ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are influenced by a multitude of parameters like concentration of TAN, air temperature, pH-value, evaporation rate, crop canopy size and complexity, crust formation, surface roughness of field, wind speed, solar radiation, total area of manure applied, application technique and infiltration to soil [31]. For the

life cycle inventory ammonia emissions are calculated dependent on the type of manure in proportion to the concentration of TAN. The TAN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. As mentioned above, ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are essentially dependent on the application technique used [31, 33, 36, 53]. If the application technique "trail hose" is used instead of the "splash plate method" ammonia emissions are assumed to be reduced by 40% [31, 33, 36, 53, 54] and for the application technique "injection (closed slot)" by 86% [33, 36, 45, 51, 53, 54].

Dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the spreading of manure are again influenced by many parameters like manuring method, soil characteristics (humidity, concen- tration of Nitrogen or humus) or climatic conditions [33]. For the calculation of the life cycle inventory the IPCC GPG [52] emission factor of 0.0196 (+0.0154) kg N2O (kg TKN) is used. The TKN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. Accord- ing to [36, 45, 51] the closed slot injection application technique increases N2O emissions by a factor of 2.95 in comparison to the "splash plate method".

In the present study the avoided burdens approach has been adopted and the conditions for the utilization of this approach listed in the introduction are met as follows:

2 The term "co-products" refers in this paper to products which are produced by the biomass-system in addition to the main product electricity and which substitute conventional products with the same function (heat and nutrients).

<list>- data for the possible substitutes are obtained from plant operators; 
- data uncertainties are not higher than for direct life cycle emissions and 
- indirect effects showed to be very important for the overall results.</list>
Where different possible substitutes were identified the effects on the total results were determined in a sensitivity analysis.

For the co-product heat for example the following assumption is made: if a usable quantity of heat produced as a co-product is used in such a way that it avoids the production of heat by more conventional means (= sub- stitute or reference system), the environmental load of the avoided, conventional heat production was subtracted from the emissions of the biomass system (= credit).

In the present study the functional output of interest is electricity. Electricity production is the main reason for the construction of the biomass plants under investigation. Additional functions provided by the different biomass systems are given in Table 2. The plants would not explicitly be built in order to provide these additional functions. Nev- ertheless they substitute processes which would otherwise be necessary to provide the additional functions if the plants did not exist (e.g. heat supply by conventional fossil fuels).

In this study the term "reference system" refers to all the processes needed for the substitution of the additional functions (e.g. conventional heat production or storage and application of undigested manure). The assumed reference systems are also given in Table 2.
As stated in the introduction, a practical problem of the avoided burdens approach is the selection of the substitutes

[4, 12-15, 23]. Information on the conventional disposal/ management of manure and organic residues used as sub- strates in the biomass-plants was obtained in personal interviews with plant operators.

For the co-product heat a fossil heat supply mix according to a study by the BMU, Germany [24] was assumed as the substitute (see "reference production heat"). According to this study, this heat supply mix rep- resents the additional fossil-based energy sources that would have to be used for heat generation if the biomass- based heat generation capacities were unavailable.
For the co-product nutrients according to the common agricultural practice conventional, synthetic N-fertilizer is assumed to be the substitute.
System Boundary

According to the ISO standards [10] the system boundary "defines the unit processes to be included in the system to be modeled". Figure 1 describes the life cycle stages considered within the system boundary. The unit processes of each life cycle stage are described in the Life Cycle Inventory, where it is also stated for which unit processes regional data has been collected and for which unit pro- cesses data from a database have been used.

Life cycle stages which cover direct life cycle emissions of electricity generation in biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of the study [7] and are shown in light collared boxes in Fig. 1. To cover indirect environmental impacts or avoided burdens from the replacement of the conventional production or provision of the additional functions the following life cycle stages have been intro- duced: "reference use land", "reference use manure",
Table 2 Additional functions of the biomass systems and their assumed substitutes (reference systems)

Additional function|Substitute (reference system)|Conversion technology Heat|Conventional fossil heat mix (REF heat)|AD, C, G Nutrients|Conventional N-fertilizer (REF fertilizer)|AD Organic residues disposal|Composting of organic residues (REF residues)|AD Manure disposal/management|Storage and application of undigested manure (REF manure)|AD
"reference use residues" "reference production heat" and "reference production fertilizer".
Life Cycle Inventory

Regional data of 34 biomass plants all over Austria were collected in personal interviews with plant operators for anaerobic digestion plants by Laaber et al. [25] and for wood-chip-CHP plants by Siegl [26]. The questionnaire was elaborated in several meetings of plant operators, plant suppliers, planners and bioenergy research representatives. It covered specific process characterizing parameters in three fields:

<list>general functional description (e.g. quality and quantity of substrates, yields, transport distances and means of transportation, substrate dosage, plant type, digestate storage type (covered/open) digestate treatment and application technique, mode of ash disposal, product gas quantity and quality, energy conversion, thermal energy use, etc.) 
measurable process conditions [e.g. COD, TS, VSS, TKN, NH4-N of substrates and digestate, CH4, CO2, H2S, O2-concentration in biogas, flue gas emissions (CO, CH4, NMVOC, NOx, particulates, SO2), etc.] 
calculable variables (e.g. substrate input in t/year, especially for AD: residence time, hydraulic load, VSS degradation, digestate production in t/year, emissions</list>
from digestate, biogas/methane yield, calorific value, etc.).

For necessary additional calculations of emissions, emission factors and calculation methods are taken from literature, which are described in the following paragraphs. Global data on environmental impacts of transport services or the production of construction materials are taken from the Ecoinvent database [27], see Fig. 1.
Direct Life Cycle Emissions
Data collection, estimations and calculation methods for the calculation of direct life cycle emissions of the different types of biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of this study [7].
Reference Use Land (REF Land)

The need for the incorporation of land use change, caused by biomass production for bioenergy, into the LCA is demonstrated by different literature sources [1, 20-22] and is mentioned in the introduction. According to Bergsma et al. [16] the cultivation of biomass for energy purposes leads to the displacement of crop production as well as the displacement of land cover prior to biomass production for energy.

Data on the alternative land and biomass use (before the introduction of the biomass-systems) have been collected in personal interviews with plant operators.

As a result of the information gathered in the interviews and case studies from literature [6, 16, 21, 28] the reference uses given in Table 3 are assumed for the different types of biomass considered within this study. The assumed refer- ence use for the alternative land use is credited to the biomass system, the assumed reference use of the alter- native biomass use is charged to the biomass system. For reasons of simplicity and better presentability of the results both reference uses for land are combined to one life cycle stage, which can be positive if the effect of the alternative biomass use prevails or negative, if the effect of alternative land use prevails.

Changes in organic carbon stocks due to land use change are especially important for green house gas balances [19,21, 22]. Depending on the earlier use of the land and the crop to be established, the land use change can be a benefit or a disad- vantage depending for example on whether a forest is con- verted to agricultural land for bioenergy production (loss of carbon stocks) or set-aside land is taken into production (carbon stock may increase) [22].

The land use changes assumed for the biomass pro- duction in this study (Table 3) are: set aside land-crop land, crop land-crop land and forest-forest. As most set- aside land in Austria is grassland that is regularly mowed or ploughed [28] and on the other hand 20% of the land that is needed for biomass production for the AD-plants investigated is used for the production of grass-silage it is assumed that no change in organic carbon stocks occurs. If the type of land does not change with the land use change again no change in the organic carbon stocks is assumed. Furthermore because changes of carbon in soil are very site-specific and highly dependent on former and current agronomic practices, climate, and soil characteristics [19]
and there are often uncertainties in the levels of carbon stock changes due to variations in local conditions and lack of reliable field trial data [21].
Reference Use Manure (REF Manure)

By the introduction of anaerobic digestion plants, which use manure as a substrate, the emissions caused by the conventional management of manure (reference use) can be avoided. Therefore, a credit for the "reference use manure" is given to the biomass conversion system.

Data on the yearly amount and type of manure used in AD plants, as well as a possible processing, storage and spreading conditions before the introduction of the AD plant are collected in interviews with plant operators. From these amounts the energy input and emissions caused by the transport to the field and the spreading process (without gaseous emissions originating from the manure itself) are calculated using the corresponding ecoinvent datasets [27].

According to Amon et al. [30] manure management, consisting of the storage and spreading of manure, is a significant source of CH4, NH3 and N2O emissions to the atmosphere. Due to a lack of measurement data, gaseous emissions from manure management are calculated via emission factors derived from different literature sources (see Table 4).

Ammonia and dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the storage occur only from the surface of stored liquid manure [31]. Ammonia emissions are dependent on physical properties like the concentration of total ammoniacal Nitrogen (TAN), dry matter or pH-value as well as envi- ronmental conditions like temperature, wind speed or precipitations [30, 32]. Dinitrogen monoxide is formed under anoxic conditions in the crust (often formed on the surface of the stored manure) when ammonium is nitrified or during the denitrification of nitrate under anaerobic

conditions [33]. For the life cycle inventory they are cal- culated depending on the manure type (cattle or swine) and storage duration in proportion to the surface area of the open storage facilities. Methane emissions from the storage are calculated dependent on the amount of manure stored and the storage duration with different emission factors for cattle and swine manure. Methane emissions from the spreading of manure are very small in comparison to emissions from storage [30]. For the life cycle inventory they are calculated dependent on the amount of manure spread. According to Sommer and Hutchings [31] gaseous emissions from agriculture, and especially from the spreading of solid and liquid manure, are one of the major sources for atmospheric ammonia in Europe. Ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are influenced by a multitude of parameters like concentration of TAN, air temperature, pH-value, evaporation rate, crop canopy size and complexity, crust formation, surface roughness of field, wind speed, solar radiation, total area of manure applied, application technique and infiltration to soil [31]. For the

life cycle inventory ammonia emissions are calculated dependent on the type of manure in proportion to the concentration of TAN. The TAN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. As mentioned above, ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are essentially dependent on the application technique used [31, 33, 36, 53]. If the application technique "trail hose" is used instead of the "splash plate method" ammonia emissions are assumed to be reduced by 40% [31, 33, 36, 53, 54] and for the application technique "injection (closed slot)" by 86% [33, 36, 45, 51, 53, 54].

Dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the spreading of manure are again influenced by many parameters like manuring method, soil characteristics (humidity, concen- tration of Nitrogen or humus) or climatic conditions [33]. For the calculation of the life cycle inventory the IPCC GPG [52] emission factor of 0.0196 (+0.0154) kg N2O (kg TKN) is used. The TKN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. Accord- ing to [36, 45, 51] the closed slot injection application technique increases N2O emissions by a factor of 2.95 in comparison to the "splash plate method".

In the present study the avoided burdens approach has been adopted and the conditions for the utilization of this approach listed in the introduction are met as follows:

2 The term "co-products" refers in this paper to products which are produced by the biomass-system in addition to the main product electricity and which substitute conventional products with the same function (heat and nutrients).

<list>- data for the possible substitutes are obtained from plant operators; 
- data uncertainties are not higher than for direct life cycle emissions and 
- indirect effects showed to be very important for the overall results.</list>
Where different possible substitutes were identified the effects on the total results were determined in a sensitivity analysis.

For the co-product heat for example the following assumption is made: if a usable quantity of heat produced as a co-product is used in such a way that it avoids the production of heat by more conventional means (= sub- stitute or reference system), the environmental load of the avoided, conventional heat production was subtracted from the emissions of the biomass system (= credit).

In the present study the functional output of interest is electricity. Electricity production is the main reason for the construction of the biomass plants under investigation. Additional functions provided by the different biomass systems are given in Table 2. The plants would not explicitly be built in order to provide these additional functions. Nev- ertheless they substitute processes which would otherwise be necessary to provide the additional functions if the plants did not exist (e.g. heat supply by conventional fossil fuels).

In this study the term "reference system" refers to all the processes needed for the substitution of the additional functions (e.g. conventional heat production or storage and application of undigested manure). The assumed reference systems are also given in Table 2.
As stated in the introduction, a practical problem of the avoided burdens approach is the selection of the substitutes

[4, 12-15, 23]. Information on the conventional disposal/ management of manure and organic residues used as sub- strates in the biomass-plants was obtained in personal interviews with plant operators.

For the co-product heat a fossil heat supply mix according to a study by the BMU, Germany [24] was assumed as the substitute (see "reference production heat"). According to this study, this heat supply mix rep- resents the additional fossil-based energy sources that would have to be used for heat generation if the biomass- based heat generation capacities were unavailable.
For the co-product nutrients according to the common agricultural practice conventional, synthetic N-fertilizer is assumed to be the substitute.
System Boundary

According to the ISO standards [10] the system boundary "defines the unit processes to be included in the system to be modeled". Figure 1 describes the life cycle stages considered within the system boundary. The unit processes of each life cycle stage are described in the Life Cycle Inventory, where it is also stated for which unit processes regional data has been collected and for which unit pro- cesses data from a database have been used.

Life cycle stages which cover direct life cycle emissions of electricity generation in biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of the study [7] and are shown in light collared boxes in Fig. 1. To cover indirect environmental impacts or avoided burdens from the replacement of the conventional production or provision of the additional functions the following life cycle stages have been intro- duced: "reference use land", "reference use manure",
Table 2 Additional functions of the biomass systems and their assumed substitutes (reference systems)

Additional function|Substitute (reference system)|Conversion technology Heat|Conventional fossil heat mix (REF heat)|AD, C, G Nutrients|Conventional N-fertilizer (REF fertilizer)|AD Organic residues disposal|Composting of organic residues (REF residues)|AD Manure disposal/management|Storage and application of undigested manure (REF manure)|AD
"reference use residues" "reference production heat" and "reference production fertilizer".
Life Cycle Inventory

Regional data of 34 biomass plants all over Austria were collected in personal interviews with plant operators for anaerobic digestion plants by Laaber et al. [25] and for wood-chip-CHP plants by Siegl [26]. The questionnaire was elaborated in several meetings of plant operators, plant suppliers, planners and bioenergy research representatives. It covered specific process characterizing parameters in three fields:

<list>general functional description (e.g. quality and quantity of substrates, yields, transport distances and means of transportation, substrate dosage, plant type, digestate storage type (covered/open) digestate treatment and application technique, mode of ash disposal, product gas quantity and quality, energy conversion, thermal energy use, etc.) 
measurable process conditions [e.g. COD, TS, VSS, TKN, NH4-N of substrates and digestate, CH4, CO2, H2S, O2-concentration in biogas, flue gas emissions (CO, CH4, NMVOC, NOx, particulates, SO2), etc.] 
calculable variables (e.g. substrate input in t/year, especially for AD: residence time, hydraulic load, VSS degradation, digestate production in t/year, emissions</list>
from digestate, biogas/methane yield, calorific value, etc.).

For necessary additional calculations of emissions, emission factors and calculation methods are taken from literature, which are described in the following paragraphs. Global data on environmental impacts of transport services or the production of construction materials are taken from the Ecoinvent database [27], see Fig. 1.
Direct Life Cycle Emissions
Data collection, estimations and calculation methods for the calculation of direct life cycle emissions of the different types of biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of this study [7].
Reference Use Land (REF Land)

The need for the incorporation of land use change, caused by biomass production for bioenergy, into the LCA is demonstrated by different literature sources [1, 20-22] and is mentioned in the introduction. According to Bergsma et al. [16] the cultivation of biomass for energy purposes leads to the displacement of crop production as well as the displacement of land cover prior to biomass production for energy.

[16] the cultivation of biomass for energy purposes leads to the displacement of crop production as well as the displacement of land cover prior to biomass production for energy.

Data on the alternative land and biomass use (before the introduction of the biomass-systems) have been collected in personal interviews with plant operators.

As a result of the information gathered in the interviews and case studies from literature [6, 16, 21, 28] the reference uses given in Table 3 are assumed for the different types of biomass considered within this study. The assumed refer- ence use for the alternative land use is credited to the biomass system, the assumed reference use of the alter- native biomass use is charged to the biomass system. For reasons of simplicity and better presentability of the results both reference uses for land are combined to one life cycle stage, which can be positive if the effect of the alternative biomass use prevails or negative, if the effect of alternative land use prevails.

Changes in organic carbon stocks due to land use change are especially important for green house gas balances [19,21, 22]. Depending on the earlier use of the land and the crop to be established, the land use change can be a benefit or a disad- vantage depending for example on whether a forest is con- verted to agricultural land for bioenergy production (loss of carbon stocks) or set-aside land is taken into production (carbon stock may increase) [22].

The land use changes assumed for the biomass pro- duction in this study (Table 3) are: set aside land-crop land, crop land-crop land and forest-forest. As most set- aside land in Austria is grassland that is regularly mowed or ploughed [28] and on the other hand 20% of the land that is needed for biomass production for the AD-plants investigated is used for the production of grass-silage it is assumed that no change in organic carbon stocks occurs. If the type of land does not change with the land use change again no change in the organic carbon stocks is assumed. Furthermore because changes of carbon in soil are very site-specific and highly dependent on former and current agronomic practices, climate, and soil characteristics [19]
and there are often uncertainties in the levels of carbon stock changes due to variations in local conditions and lack of reliable field trial data [21].
Reference Use Manure (REF Manure)

By the introduction of anaerobic digestion plants, which use manure as a substrate, the emissions caused by the conventional management of manure (reference use) can be avoided. Therefore, a credit for the "reference use manure" is given to the biomass conversion system.

Data on the yearly amount and type of manure used in AD plants, as well as a possible processing, storage and spreading conditions before the introduction of the AD plant are collected in interviews with plant operators. From these amounts the energy input and emissions caused by the transport to the field and the spreading process (without gaseous emissions originating from the manure itself) are calculated using the corresponding ecoinvent datasets [27].

According to Amon et al. [30] manure management, consisting of the storage and spreading of manure, is a significant source of CH4, NH3 and N2O emissions to the atmosphere. Due to a lack of measurement data, gaseous emissions from manure management are calculated via emission factors derived from different literature sources (see Table 4).

Ammonia and dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the storage occur only from the surface of stored liquid manure [31]. Ammonia emissions are dependent on physical properties like the concentration of total ammoniacal Nitrogen (TAN), dry matter or pH-value as well as envi- ronmental conditions like temperature, wind speed or precipitations [30, 32]. Dinitrogen monoxide is formed under anoxic conditions in the crust (often formed on the surface of the stored manure) when ammonium is nitrified or during the denitrification of nitrate under anaerobic

conditions [33]. For the life cycle inventory they are cal- culated depending on the manure type (cattle or swine) and storage duration in proportion to the surface area of the open storage facilities. Methane emissions from the storage are calculated dependent on the amount of manure stored and the storage duration with different emission factors for cattle and swine manure. Methane emissions from the spreading of manure are very small in comparison to emissions from storage [30]. For the life cycle inventory they are calculated dependent on the amount of manure spread. According to Sommer and Hutchings [31] gaseous emissions from agriculture, and especially from the spreading of solid and liquid manure, are one of the major sources for atmospheric ammonia in Europe. Ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are influenced by a multitude of parameters like concentration of TAN, air temperature, pH-value, evaporation rate, crop canopy size and complexity, crust formation, surface roughness of field, wind speed, solar radiation, total area of manure applied, application technique and infiltration to soil [31]. For the

life cycle inventory ammonia emissions are calculated dependent on the type of manure in proportion to the concentration of TAN. The TAN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. As mentioned above, ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are essentially dependent on the application technique used [31, 33, 36, 53]. If the application technique "trail hose" is used instead of the "splash plate method" ammonia emissions are assumed to be reduced by 40% [31, 33, 36, 53, 54] and for the application technique "injection (closed slot)" by 86% [33, 36, 45, 51, 53, 54].

Dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the spreading of manure are again influenced by many parameters like manuring method, soil characteristics (humidity, concen- tration of Nitrogen or humus) or climatic conditions [33]. For the calculation of the life cycle inventory the IPCC GPG [52] emission factor of 0.0196 (+0.0154) kg N2O (kg TKN) is used. The TKN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. Accord- ing to [36, 45, 51] the closed slot injection application technique increases N2O emissions by a factor of 2.95 in comparison to the "splash plate method".

In the present study the avoided burdens approach has been adopted and the conditions for the utilization of this approach listed in the introduction are met as follows:

<list>- data for the possible substitutes are obtained from plant operators; 
- data uncertainties are not higher than for direct life cycle emissions and 
- indirect effects showed to be very important for the overall results.</list>
Where different possible substitutes were identified the effects on the total results were determined in a sensitivity analysis.

For the co-product heat for example the following assumption is made: if a usable quantity of heat produced as a co-product is used in such a way that it avoids the production of heat by more conventional means (= sub- stitute or reference system), the environmental load of the avoided, conventional heat production was subtracted from the emissions of the biomass system (= credit).

In the present study the functional output of interest is electricity. Electricity production is the main reason for the construction of the biomass plants under investigation. Additional functions provided by the different biomass systems are given in Table 2. The plants would not explicitly be built in order to provide these additional functions. Nev- ertheless they substitute processes which would otherwise be necessary to provide the additional functions if the plants did not exist (e.g. heat supply by conventional fossil fuels).

In this study the term "reference system" refers to all the processes needed for the substitution of the additional functions (e.g. conventional heat production or storage and application of undigested manure). The assumed reference systems are also given in Table 2.
As stated in the introduction, a practical problem of the avoided burdens approach is the selection of the substitutes

[4, 12-15, 23]. Information on the conventional disposal/ management of manure and organic residues used as sub- strates in the biomass-plants was obtained in personal interviews with plant operators.

For the co-product heat a fossil heat supply mix according to a study by the BMU, Germany [24] was assumed as the substitute (see "reference production heat"). According to this study, this heat supply mix rep- resents the additional fossil-based energy sources that would have to be used for heat generation if the biomass- based heat generation capacities were unavailable.
For the co-product nutrients according to the common agricultural practice conventional, synthetic N-fertilizer is assumed to be the substitute.
System Boundary

According to the ISO standards [10] the system boundary "defines the unit processes to be included in the system to be modeled". Figure 1 describes the life cycle stages considered within the system boundary. The unit processes of each life cycle stage are described in the Life Cycle Inventory, where it is also stated for which unit processes regional data has been collected and for which unit pro- cesses data from a database have been used.

Life cycle stages which cover direct life cycle emissions of electricity generation in biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of the study [7] and are shown in light collared boxes in Fig. 1. To cover indirect environmental impacts or avoided burdens from the replacement of the conventional production or provision of the additional functions the following life cycle stages have been intro- duced: "reference use land", "reference use manure",
