Life Cycle Inventory

Regional data of 34 biomass plants all over Austria were collected in personal interviews with plant operators for anaerobic digestion plants by Laaber et al. [25] and for wood-chip-CHP plants by Siegl [26]. The questionnaire was elaborated in several meetings of plant operators, plant suppliers, planners and bioenergy research representatives. It covered specific process characterizing parameters in three fields:

<list>general functional description (e.g. quality and quantity of substrates, yields, transport distances and means of transportation, substrate dosage, plant type, digestate storage type (covered/open) digestate treatment and application technique, mode of ash disposal, product gas quantity and quality, energy conversion, thermal energy use, etc.) 
measurable process conditions [e.g. COD, TS, VSS, TKN, NH4-N of substrates and digestate, CH4, CO2, H2S, O2-concentration in biogas, flue gas emissions (CO, CH4, NMVOC, NOx, particulates, SO2), etc.] 
calculable variables (e.g. substrate input in t/year, especially for AD: residence time, hydraulic load, VSS degradation, digestate production in t/year, emissions</list>
from digestate, biogas/methane yield, calorific value, etc.).

For necessary additional calculations of emissions, emission factors and calculation methods are taken from literature, which are described in the following paragraphs. Global data on environmental impacts of transport services or the production of construction materials are taken from the Ecoinvent database [27], see Fig. 1.

Data collection, estimations and calculation methods for the calculation of direct life cycle emissions of the different types of biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of this study [7].
Reference Use Land (REF Land)

The need for the incorporation of land use change, caused by biomass production for bioenergy, into the LCA is demonstrated by different literature sources [1, 20-22] and is mentioned in the introduction. According to Bergsma et al. [16] the cultivation of biomass for energy purposes leads to the displacement of crop production as well as the displacement of land cover prior to biomass production for energy.

Data on the alternative land and biomass use (before the introduction of the biomass-systems) have been collected in personal interviews with plant operators.

As a result of the information gathered in the interviews and case studies from literature [6, 16, 21, 28] the reference uses given in Table 3 are assumed for the different types of biomass considered within this study. The assumed refer- ence use for the alternative land use is credited to the biomass system, the assumed reference use of the alter- native biomass use is charged to the biomass system. For reasons of simplicity and better presentability of the results both reference uses for land are combined to one life cycle stage, which can be positive if the effect of the alternative biomass use prevails or negative, if the effect of alternative land use prevails.

Changes in organic carbon stocks due to land use change are especially important for green house gas balances [19,21, 22]. Depending on the earlier use of the land and the crop to be established, the land use change can be a benefit or a disad- vantage depending for example on whether a forest is con- verted to agricultural land for bioenergy production (loss of carbon stocks) or set-aside land is taken into production (carbon stock may increase) [22].

The land use changes assumed for the biomass pro- duction in this study (Table 3) are: set aside land-crop land, crop land-crop land and forest-forest. As most set- aside land in Austria is grassland that is regularly mowed or ploughed [28] and on the other hand 20% of the land that is needed for biomass production for the AD-plants investigated is used for the production of grass-silage it is assumed that no change in organic carbon stocks occurs. If the type of land does not change with the land use change again no change in the organic carbon stocks is assumed. Furthermore because changes of carbon in soil are very site-specific and highly dependent on former and current agronomic practices, climate, and soil characteristics [19]
and there are often uncertainties in the levels of carbon stock changes due to variations in local conditions and lack of reliable field trial data [21].
Reference Use Manure (REF Manure)

By the introduction of anaerobic digestion plants, which use manure as a substrate, the emissions caused by the conventional management of manure (reference use) can be avoided. Therefore, a credit for the "reference use manure" is given to the biomass conversion system.

Data on the yearly amount and type of manure used in AD plants, as well as a possible processing, storage and spreading conditions before the introduction of the AD plant are collected in interviews with plant operators. From these amounts the energy input and emissions caused by the transport to the field and the spreading process (without gaseous emissions originating from the manure itself) are calculated using the corresponding ecoinvent datasets [27].

According to Amon et al. [30] manure management, consisting of the storage and spreading of manure, is a significant source of CH4, NH3 and N2O emissions to the atmosphere. Due to a lack of measurement data, gaseous emissions from manure management are calculated via emission factors derived from different literature sources (see Table 4).

Ammonia and dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the storage occur only from the surface of stored liquid manure [31]. Ammonia emissions are dependent on physical properties like the concentration of total ammoniacal Nitrogen (TAN), dry matter or pH-value as well as envi- ronmental conditions like temperature, wind speed or precipitations [30, 32]. Dinitrogen monoxide is formed under anoxic conditions in the crust (often formed on the surface of the stored manure) when ammonium is nitrified or during the denitrification of nitrate under anaerobic

conditions [33]. For the life cycle inventory they are cal- culated depending on the manure type (cattle or swine) and storage duration in proportion to the surface area of the open storage facilities. Methane emissions from the storage are calculated dependent on the amount of manure stored and the storage duration with different emission factors for cattle and swine manure. Methane emissions from the spreading of manure are very small in comparison to emissions from storage [30]. For the life cycle inventory they are calculated dependent on the amount of manure spread. According to Sommer and Hutchings [31] gaseous emissions from agriculture, and especially from the spreading of solid and liquid manure, are one of the major sources for atmospheric ammonia in Europe. Ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are influenced by a multitude of parameters like concentration of TAN, air temperature, pH-value, evaporation rate, crop canopy size and complexity, crust formation, surface roughness of field, wind speed, solar radiation, total area of manure applied, application technique and infiltration to soil [31]. For the

life cycle inventory ammonia emissions are calculated dependent on the type of manure in proportion to the concentration of TAN. The TAN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. As mentioned above, ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are essentially dependent on the application technique used [31, 33, 36, 53]. If the application technique "trail hose" is used instead of the "splash plate method" ammonia emissions are assumed to be reduced by 40% [31, 33, 36, 53, 54] and for the application technique "injection (closed slot)" by 86% [33, 36, 45, 51, 53, 54].

Dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the spreading of manure are again influenced by many parameters like manuring method, soil characteristics (humidity, concen- tration of Nitrogen or humus) or climatic conditions [33]. For the calculation of the life cycle inventory the IPCC GPG [52] emission factor of 0.0196 (+0.0154) kg N2O (kg TKN) is used. The TKN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. Accord- ing to [36, 45, 51] the closed slot injection application technique increases N2O emissions by a factor of 2.95 in comparison to the "splash plate method".

In the present study the avoided burdens approach has been adopted and the conditions for the utilization of this approach listed in the introduction are met as follows:

<list>- data for the possible substitutes are obtained from plant operators; 
- data uncertainties are not higher than for direct life cycle emissions and 
- indirect effects showed to be very important for the overall results.</list>
Where different possible substitutes were identified the effects on the total results were determined in a sensitivity analysis.

For the co-product heat for example the following assumption is made: if a usable quantity of heat produced as a co-product is used in such a way that it avoids the production of heat by more conventional means (= sub- stitute or reference system), the environmental load of the avoided, conventional heat production was subtracted from the emissions of the biomass system (= credit).

In the present study the functional output of interest is electricity. Electricity production is the main reason for the construction of the biomass plants under investigation. Additional functions provided by the different biomass systems are given in Table 2. The plants would not explicitly be built in order to provide these additional functions. Nev- ertheless they substitute processes which would otherwise be necessary to provide the additional functions if the plants did not exist (e.g. heat supply by conventional fossil fuels).

In this study the term "reference system" refers to all the processes needed for the substitution of the additional functions (e.g. conventional heat production or storage and application of undigested manure). The assumed reference systems are also given in Table 2.
As stated in the introduction, a practical problem of the avoided burdens approach is the selection of the substitutes

[4, 12-15, 23]. Information on the conventional disposal/ management of manure and organic residues used as sub- strates in the biomass-plants was obtained in personal interviews with plant operators.

For the co-product heat a fossil heat supply mix according to a study by the BMU, Germany [24] was assumed as the substitute (see "reference production heat"). According to this study, this heat supply mix rep- resents the additional fossil-based energy sources that would have to be used for heat generation if the biomass- based heat generation capacities were unavailable.
For the co-product nutrients according to the common agricultural practice conventional, synthetic N-fertilizer is assumed to be the substitute.
System Boundary

According to the ISO standards [10] the system boundary "defines the unit processes to be included in the system to be modeled". Figure 1 describes the life cycle stages considered within the system boundary. The unit processes of each life cycle stage are described in the Life Cycle Inventory, where it is also stated for which unit processes regional data has been collected and for which unit pro- cesses data from a database have been used.

Life cycle stages which cover direct life cycle emissions of electricity generation in biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of the study [7] and are shown in light collared boxes in Fig. 1. To cover indirect environmental impacts or avoided burdens from the replacement of the conventional production or provision of the additional functions the following life cycle stages have been intro- duced: "reference use land", "reference use manure",

Life Cycle Inventory

It covered specific process characterizing parameters in three fields:

<list>general functional description (e.g. quality and quantity of substrates, yields, transport distances and means of transportation, substrate dosage, plant type, digestate storage type (covered/open) digestate treatment and application technique, mode of ash disposal, product gas quantity and quality, energy conversion, thermal energy use, etc.) 
measurable process conditions [e.g. COD, TS, VSS, TKN, NH4-N of substrates and digestate, CH4, CO2, H2S, O2-concentration in biogas, flue gas emissions (CO, CH4, NMVOC, NOx, particulates, SO2), etc.] 
calculable variables (e.g. substrate input in t/year, especially for AD: residence time, hydraulic load, VSS degradation, digestate production in t/year, emissions</list>
from digestate, biogas/methane yield, calorific value, etc.).

For necessary additional calculations of emissions, emission factors and calculation methods are taken from literature, which are described in the following paragraphs. Global data on environmental impacts of transport services or the production of construction materials are taken from the Ecoinvent database [27], see Fig. 1.

For necessary additional calculations of emissions, emission factors and calculation methods are taken from literature, which are described in the following paragraphs. Global data on environmental impacts of transport services or the production of construction materials are taken from the Ecoinvent database [27], see Fig. 1.
Direct Life Cycle Emissions
Data collection, estimations and calculation methods for the calculation of direct life cycle emissions of the different types of biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of this study [7].
Reference Use Land (REF Land)

The need for the incorporation of land use change, caused by biomass production for bioenergy, into the LCA is demonstrated by different literature sources [1, 20-22] and is mentioned in the introduction. According to Bergsma et al. [16] the cultivation of biomass for energy purposes leads to the displacement of crop production as well as the displacement of land cover prior to biomass production for energy.

Data on the alternative land and biomass use (before the introduction of the biomass-systems) have been collected in personal interviews with plant operators.

As a result of the information gathered in the interviews and case studies from literature [6, 16, 21, 28] the reference uses given in Table 3 are assumed for the different types of biomass considered within this study. The assumed refer- ence use for the alternative land use is credited to the biomass system, the assumed reference use of the alter- native biomass use is charged to the biomass system. For reasons of simplicity and better presentability of the results both reference uses for land are combined to one life cycle stage, which can be positive if the effect of the alternative biomass use prevails or negative, if the effect of alternative land use prevails.

Changes in organic carbon stocks due to land use change are especially important for green house gas balances [19,21, 22]. Depending on the earlier use of the land and the crop to be established, the land use change can be a benefit or a disad- vantage depending for example on whether a forest is con- verted to agricultural land for bioenergy production (loss of carbon stocks) or set-aside land is taken into production (carbon stock may increase) [22].

The land use changes assumed for the biomass pro- duction in this study (Table 3) are: set aside land-crop land, crop land-crop land and forest-forest. As most set- aside land in Austria is grassland that is regularly mowed or ploughed [28] and on the other hand 20% of the land that is needed for biomass production for the AD-plants investigated is used for the production of grass-silage it is assumed that no change in organic carbon stocks occurs. If the type of land does not change with the land use change again no change in the organic carbon stocks is assumed. Furthermore because changes of carbon in soil are very site-specific and highly dependent on former and current agronomic practices, climate, and soil characteristics [19]
and there are often uncertainties in the levels of carbon stock changes due to variations in local conditions and lack of reliable field trial data [21].
Reference Use Manure (REF Manure)

By the introduction of anaerobic digestion plants, which use manure as a substrate, the emissions caused by the conventional management of manure (reference use) can be avoided. Therefore, a credit for the "reference use manure" is given to the biomass conversion system.

Data on the yearly amount and type of manure used in AD plants, as well as a possible processing, storage and spreading conditions before the introduction of the AD plant are collected in interviews with plant operators. From these amounts the energy input and emissions caused by the transport to the field and the spreading process (without gaseous emissions originating from the manure itself) are calculated using the corresponding ecoinvent datasets [27].

According to Amon et al. [30] manure management, consisting of the storage and spreading of manure, is a significant source of CH4, NH3 and N2O emissions to the atmosphere. Due to a lack of measurement data, gaseous emissions from manure management are calculated via emission factors derived from different literature sources (see Table 4).

Ammonia and dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the storage occur only from the surface of stored liquid manure [31]. Ammonia emissions are dependent on physical properties like the concentration of total ammoniacal Nitrogen (TAN), dry matter or pH-value as well as envi- ronmental conditions like temperature, wind speed or precipitations [30, 32]. Dinitrogen monoxide is formed under anoxic conditions in the crust (often formed on the surface of the stored manure) when ammonium is nitrified or during the denitrification of nitrate under anaerobic

conditions [33]. For the life cycle inventory they are cal- culated depending on the manure type (cattle or swine) and storage duration in proportion to the surface area of the open storage facilities. Methane emissions from the storage are calculated dependent on the amount of manure stored and the storage duration with different emission factors for cattle and swine manure. Methane emissions from the spreading of manure are very small in comparison to emissions from storage [30]. For the life cycle inventory they are calculated dependent on the amount of manure spread. According to Sommer and Hutchings [31] gaseous emissions from agriculture, and especially from the spreading of solid and liquid manure, are one of the major sources for atmospheric ammonia in Europe. Ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are influenced by a multitude of parameters like concentration of TAN, air temperature, pH-value, evaporation rate, crop canopy size and complexity, crust formation, surface roughness of field, wind speed, solar radiation, total area of manure applied, application technique and infiltration to soil [31]. For the

life cycle inventory ammonia emissions are calculated dependent on the type of manure in proportion to the concentration of TAN. The TAN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. As mentioned above, ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are essentially dependent on the application technique used [31, 33, 36, 53]. If the application technique "trail hose" is used instead of the "splash plate method" ammonia emissions are assumed to be reduced by 40% [31, 33, 36, 53, 54] and for the application technique "injection (closed slot)" by 86% [33, 36, 45, 51, 53, 54].

Dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the spreading of manure are again influenced by many parameters like manuring method, soil characteristics (humidity, concen- tration of Nitrogen or humus) or climatic conditions [33]. For the calculation of the life cycle inventory the IPCC GPG [52] emission factor of 0.0196 (+0.0154) kg N2O (kg TKN) is used. The TKN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. Accord- ing to [36, 45, 51] the closed slot injection application technique increases N2O emissions by a factor of 2.95 in comparison to the "splash plate method".

Where different possible substitutes were identified the effects on the total results were determined in a sensitivity analysis.

For the co-product heat for example the following assumption is made: if a usable quantity of heat produced as a co-product is used in such a way that it avoids the production of heat by more conventional means (= sub- stitute or reference system), the environmental load of the avoided, conventional heat production was subtracted from the emissions of the biomass system (= credit).

In the present study the functional output of interest is electricity. Electricity production is the main reason for the construction of the biomass plants under investigation. Additional functions provided by the different biomass systems are given in Table 2. The plants would not explicitly be built in order to provide these additional functions. Nev- ertheless they substitute processes which would otherwise be necessary to provide the additional functions if the plants did not exist (e.g. heat supply by conventional fossil fuels).

In this study the term "reference system" refers to all the processes needed for the substitution of the additional functions (e.g. conventional heat production or storage and application of undigested manure). The assumed reference systems are also given in Table 2.
As stated in the introduction, a practical problem of the avoided burdens approach is the selection of the substitutes

[4, 12-15, 23]. Information on the conventional disposal/ management of manure and organic residues used as sub- strates in the biomass-plants was obtained in personal interviews with plant operators.

For the co-product heat a fossil heat supply mix according to a study by the BMU, Germany [24] was assumed as the substitute (see "reference production heat"). According to this study, this heat supply mix rep- resents the additional fossil-based energy sources that would have to be used for heat generation if the biomass- based heat generation capacities were unavailable.
For the co-product nutrients according to the common agricultural practice conventional, synthetic N-fertilizer is assumed to be the substitute.
System Boundary

According to the ISO standards [10] the system boundary "defines the unit processes to be included in the system to be modeled". Figure 1 describes the life cycle stages considered within the system boundary. The unit processes of each life cycle stage are described in the Life Cycle Inventory, where it is also stated for which unit processes regional data has been collected and for which unit pro- cesses data from a database have been used.

Life cycle stages which cover direct life cycle emissions of electricity generation in biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of the study [7] and are shown in light collared boxes in Fig. 1. To cover indirect environmental impacts or avoided burdens from the replacement of the conventional production or provision of the additional functions the following life cycle stages have been intro- duced: "reference use land", "reference use manure",

measurable process conditions [e.g. COD, TS, VSS, TKN, NH4-N of substrates and digestate, CH4, CO2, H2S, O2-concentration in biogas, flue gas emissions (CO, CH4, NMVOC, NOx, particulates, SO2), etc.] 
calculable variables (e.g. substrate input in t/year, especially for AD: residence time, hydraulic load, VSS degradation, digestate production in t/year, emissions</list>
from digestate, biogas/methane yield, calorific value, etc.).

For necessary additional calculations of emissions, emission factors and calculation methods are taken from literature, which are described in the following paragraphs. Global data on environmental impacts of transport services or the production of construction materials are taken from the Ecoinvent database [27], see Fig. 1.
Direct Life Cycle Emissions
Data collection, estimations and calculation methods for the calculation of direct life cycle emissions of the different types of biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of this study [7].
Reference Use Land (REF Land)

The need for the incorporation of land use change, caused by biomass production for bioenergy, into the LCA is demonstrated by different literature sources [1, 20-22] and is mentioned in the introduction. According to Bergsma et al. [16] the cultivation of biomass for energy purposes leads to the displacement of crop production as well as the displacement of land cover prior to biomass production for energy.

Data on the alternative land and biomass use (before the introduction of the biomass-systems) have been collected in personal interviews with plant operators.

As a result of the information gathered in the interviews and case studies from literature [6, 16, 21, 28] the reference uses given in Table 3 are assumed for the different types of biomass considered within this study. The assumed refer- ence use for the alternative land use is credited to the biomass system, the assumed reference use of the alter- native biomass use is charged to the biomass system. For reasons of simplicity and better presentability of the results both reference uses for land are combined to one life cycle stage, which can be positive if the effect of the alternative biomass use prevails or negative, if the effect of alternative land use prevails.

Changes in organic carbon stocks due to land use change are especially important for green house gas balances [19,21, 22]. Depending on the earlier use of the land and the crop to be established, the land use change can be a benefit or a disad- vantage depending for example on whether a forest is con- verted to agricultural land for bioenergy production (loss of carbon stocks) or set-aside land is taken into production (carbon stock may increase) [22].

The land use changes assumed for the biomass pro- duction in this study (Table 3) are: set aside land-crop land, crop land-crop land and forest-forest. As most set- aside land in Austria is grassland that is regularly mowed or ploughed [28] and on the other hand 20% of the land that is needed for biomass production for the AD-plants investigated is used for the production of grass-silage it is assumed that no change in organic carbon stocks occurs. If the type of land does not change with the land use change again no change in the organic carbon stocks is assumed. Furthermore because changes of carbon in soil are very site-specific and highly dependent on former and current agronomic practices, climate, and soil characteristics [19]
and there are often uncertainties in the levels of carbon stock changes due to variations in local conditions and lack of reliable field trial data [21].
Reference Use Manure (REF Manure)

By the introduction of anaerobic digestion plants, which use manure as a substrate, the emissions caused by the conventional management of manure (reference use) can be avoided. Therefore, a credit for the "reference use manure" is given to the biomass conversion system.

Data on the yearly amount and type of manure used in AD plants, as well as a possible processing, storage and spreading conditions before the introduction of the AD plant are collected in interviews with plant operators. From these amounts the energy input and emissions caused by the transport to the field and the spreading process (without gaseous emissions originating from the manure itself) are calculated using the corresponding ecoinvent datasets [27].

According to Amon et al. [30] manure management, consisting of the storage and spreading of manure, is a significant source of CH4, NH3 and N2O emissions to the atmosphere. Due to a lack of measurement data, gaseous emissions from manure management are calculated via emission factors derived from different literature sources (see Table 4).

Ammonia and dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the storage occur only from the surface of stored liquid manure [31]. Ammonia emissions are dependent on physical properties like the concentration of total ammoniacal Nitrogen (TAN), dry matter or pH-value as well as envi- ronmental conditions like temperature, wind speed or precipitations [30, 32]. Dinitrogen monoxide is formed under anoxic conditions in the crust (often formed on the surface of the stored manure) when ammonium is nitrified or during the denitrification of nitrate under anaerobic

conditions [33]. For the life cycle inventory they are cal- culated depending on the manure type (cattle or swine) and storage duration in proportion to the surface area of the open storage facilities. Methane emissions from the storage are calculated dependent on the amount of manure stored and the storage duration with different emission factors for cattle and swine manure. Methane emissions from the spreading of manure are very small in comparison to emissions from storage [30]. For the life cycle inventory they are calculated dependent on the amount of manure spread. According to Sommer and Hutchings [31] gaseous emissions from agriculture, and especially from the spreading of solid and liquid manure, are one of the major sources for atmospheric ammonia in Europe. Ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are influenced by a multitude of parameters like concentration of TAN, air temperature, pH-value, evaporation rate, crop canopy size and complexity, crust formation, surface roughness of field, wind speed, solar radiation, total area of manure applied, application technique and infiltration to soil [31]. For the

life cycle inventory ammonia emissions are calculated dependent on the type of manure in proportion to the concentration of TAN. The TAN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. As mentioned above, ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are essentially dependent on the application technique used [31, 33, 36, 53]. If the application technique "trail hose" is used instead of the "splash plate method" ammonia emissions are assumed to be reduced by 40% [31, 33, 36, 53, 54] and for the application technique "injection (closed slot)" by 86% [33, 36, 45, 51, 53, 54].

Dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the spreading of manure are again influenced by many parameters like manuring method, soil characteristics (humidity, concen- tration of Nitrogen or humus) or climatic conditions [33]. For the calculation of the life cycle inventory the IPCC GPG [52] emission factor of 0.0196 (+0.0154) kg N2O (kg TKN) is used. The TKN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. Accord- ing to [36, 45, 51] the closed slot injection application technique increases N2O emissions by a factor of 2.95 in comparison to the "splash plate method".

<list>- data for the possible substitutes are obtained from plant operators; 
- data uncertainties are not higher than for direct life cycle emissions and

Where different possible substitutes were identified the effects on the total results were determined in a sensitivity analysis.

For the co-product heat for example the following assumption is made: if a usable quantity of heat produced as a co-product is used in such a way that it avoids the production of heat by more conventional means (= sub- stitute or reference system), the environmental load of the avoided, conventional heat production was subtracted from the emissions of the biomass system (= credit).

In the present study the functional output of interest is electricity. Electricity production is the main reason for the construction of the biomass plants under investigation. Additional functions provided by the different biomass systems are given in Table 2. The plants would not explicitly be built in order to provide these additional functions. Nev- ertheless they substitute processes which would otherwise be necessary to provide the additional functions if the plants did not exist (e.g. heat supply by conventional fossil fuels).

In this study the term "reference system" refers to all the processes needed for the substitution of the additional functions (e.g. conventional heat production or storage and application of undigested manure). The assumed reference systems are also given in Table 2.
As stated in the introduction, a practical problem of the avoided burdens approach is the selection of the substitutes

[4, 12-15, 23]. Information on the conventional disposal/ management of manure and organic residues used as sub- strates in the biomass-plants was obtained in personal interviews with plant operators.

For the co-product heat a fossil heat supply mix according to a study by the BMU, Germany [24] was assumed as the substitute (see "reference production heat"). According to this study, this heat supply mix rep- resents the additional fossil-based energy sources that would have to be used for heat generation if the biomass- based heat generation capacities were unavailable.
For the co-product nutrients according to the common agricultural practice conventional, synthetic N-fertilizer is assumed to be the substitute.
System Boundary

According to the ISO standards [10] the system boundary "defines the unit processes to be included in the system to be modeled". Figure 1 describes the life cycle stages considered within the system boundary. The unit processes of each life cycle stage are described in the Life Cycle Inventory, where it is also stated for which unit processes regional data has been collected and for which unit pro- cesses data from a database have been used.

Life cycle stages which cover direct life cycle emissions of electricity generation in biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of the study [7] and are shown in light collared boxes in Fig. 1. To cover indirect environmental impacts or avoided burdens from the replacement of the conventional production or provision of the additional functions the following life cycle stages have been intro- duced: "reference use land", "reference use manure",

[25] and for wood-chip-CHP plants by Siegl [26]. The questionnaire was elaborated in several meetings of plant operators, plant suppliers, planners and bioenergy research representatives. It covered specific process characterizing parameters in three fields:

<list>general functional description (e.g. quality and quantity of substrates, yields, transport distances and means of transportation, substrate dosage, plant type, digestate storage type (covered/open) digestate treatment and application technique, mode of ash disposal, product gas quantity and quality, energy conversion, thermal energy use, etc.) 
measurable process conditions [e.g. COD, TS, VSS, TKN, NH4-N of substrates and digestate, CH4, CO2, H2S, O2-concentration in biogas, flue gas emissions (CO, CH4, NMVOC, NOx, particulates, SO2), etc.] 
calculable variables (e.g. substrate input in t/year, especially for AD: residence time, hydraulic load, VSS degradation, digestate production in t/year, emissions</list>
from digestate, biogas/methane yield, calorific value, etc.).

For necessary additional calculations of emissions, emission factors and calculation methods are taken from literature, which are described in the following paragraphs. Global data on environmental impacts of transport services or the production of construction materials are taken from the Ecoinvent database [27], see Fig. 1.
Direct Life Cycle Emissions
Data collection, estimations and calculation methods for the calculation of direct life cycle emissions of the different types of biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of this study [7].
Reference Use Land (REF Land)

The need for the incorporation of land use change, caused by biomass production for bioenergy, into the LCA is demonstrated by different literature sources [1, 20-22] and is mentioned in the introduction. According to Bergsma et al. [16] the cultivation of biomass for energy purposes leads to the displacement of crop production as well as the displacement of land cover prior to biomass production for energy.

Life cycle stages covering avoided environmental burdens from the conventional provision of additional functions of the biomass systems (= reference systems) are shown in grey boxes. Life cycle stages considered for the life cycle assessment of direct life cycle emissions from biomass plants for electricity generation (described in Part I [7]) are shown in light-collared boxes

Data on the alternative land and biomass use (before the introduction of the biomass-systems) have been collected in personal interviews with plant operators.

As a result of the information gathered in the interviews and case studies from literature [6, 16, 21, 28] the reference uses given in Table 3 are assumed for the different types of biomass considered within this study. The assumed refer- ence use for the alternative land use is credited to the biomass system, the assumed reference use of the alter- native biomass use is charged to the biomass system. For reasons of simplicity and better presentability of the results both reference uses for land are combined to one life cycle stage, which can be positive if the effect of the alternative biomass use prevails or negative, if the effect of alternative land use prevails.

Changes in organic carbon stocks due to land use change are especially important for green house gas balances [19,21, 22]. Depending on the earlier use of the land and the crop to be established, the land use change can be a benefit or a disad- vantage depending for example on whether a forest is con- verted to agricultural land for bioenergy production (loss of carbon stocks) or set-aside land is taken into production (carbon stock may increase) [22].

The land use changes assumed for the biomass pro- duction in this study (Table 3) are: set aside land-crop land, crop land-crop land and forest-forest. As most set- aside land in Austria is grassland that is regularly mowed or ploughed [28] and on the other hand 20% of the land that is needed for biomass production for the AD-plants investigated is used for the production of grass-silage it is assumed that no change in organic carbon stocks occurs. If the type of land does not change with the land use change again no change in the organic carbon stocks is assumed. Furthermore because changes of carbon in soil are very site-specific and highly dependent on former and current agronomic practices, climate, and soil characteristics [19]
and there are often uncertainties in the levels of carbon stock changes due to variations in local conditions and lack of reliable field trial data [21].
Reference Use Manure (REF Manure)

By the introduction of anaerobic digestion plants, which use manure as a substrate, the emissions caused by the conventional management of manure (reference use) can be avoided. Therefore, a credit for the "reference use manure" is given to the biomass conversion system.

Data on the yearly amount and type of manure used in AD plants, as well as a possible processing, storage and spreading conditions before the introduction of the AD plant are collected in interviews with plant operators. From these amounts the energy input and emissions caused by the transport to the field and the spreading process (without gaseous emissions originating from the manure itself) are calculated using the corresponding ecoinvent datasets [27].

According to Amon et al. [30] manure management, consisting of the storage and spreading of manure, is a significant source of CH4, NH3 and N2O emissions to the atmosphere. Due to a lack of measurement data, gaseous emissions from manure management are calculated via emission factors derived from different literature sources (see Table 4).

Ammonia and dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the storage occur only from the surface of stored liquid manure [31]. Ammonia emissions are dependent on physical properties like the concentration of total ammoniacal Nitrogen (TAN), dry matter or pH-value as well as envi- ronmental conditions like temperature, wind speed or precipitations [30, 32]. Dinitrogen monoxide is formed under anoxic conditions in the crust (often formed on the surface of the stored manure) when ammonium is nitrified or during the denitrification of nitrate under anaerobic

conditions [33]. For the life cycle inventory they are cal- culated depending on the manure type (cattle or swine) and storage duration in proportion to the surface area of the open storage facilities. Methane emissions from the storage are calculated dependent on the amount of manure stored and the storage duration with different emission factors for cattle and swine manure. Methane emissions from the spreading of manure are very small in comparison to emissions from storage [30]. For the life cycle inventory they are calculated dependent on the amount of manure spread. According to Sommer and Hutchings [31] gaseous emissions from agriculture, and especially from the spreading of solid and liquid manure, are one of the major sources for atmospheric ammonia in Europe. Ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are influenced by a multitude of parameters like concentration of TAN, air temperature, pH-value, evaporation rate, crop canopy size and complexity, crust formation, surface roughness of field, wind speed, solar radiation, total area of manure applied, application technique and infiltration to soil [31]. For the

life cycle inventory ammonia emissions are calculated dependent on the type of manure in proportion to the concentration of TAN. The TAN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. As mentioned above, ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are essentially dependent on the application technique used [31, 33, 36, 53]. If the application technique "trail hose" is used instead of the "splash plate method" ammonia emissions are assumed to be reduced by 40% [31, 33, 36, 53, 54] and for the application technique "injection (closed slot)" by 86% [33, 36, 45, 51, 53, 54].

Dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the spreading of manure are again influenced by many parameters like manuring method, soil characteristics (humidity, concen- tration of Nitrogen or humus) or climatic conditions [33]. For the calculation of the life cycle inventory the IPCC GPG [52] emission factor of 0.0196 (+0.0154) kg N2O (kg TKN) is used. The TKN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. Accord- ing to [36, 45, 51] the closed slot injection application technique increases N2O emissions by a factor of 2.95 in comparison to the "splash plate method".

In the present study the avoided burdens approach has been adopted and the conditions for the utilization of this approach listed in the introduction are met as follows:

<list>- data for the possible substitutes are obtained from plant operators; 
- data uncertainties are not higher than for direct life cycle emissions and 
- indirect effects showed to be very important for the overall results.</list>
Where different possible substitutes were identified the effects on the total results were determined in a sensitivity analysis.

For the co-product heat for example the following assumption is made: if a usable quantity of heat produced as a co-product is used in such a way that it avoids the production of heat by more conventional means (= sub- stitute or reference system), the environmental load of the avoided, conventional heat production was subtracted from the emissions of the biomass system (= credit).

In the present study the functional output of interest is electricity. Electricity production is the main reason for the construction of the biomass plants under investigation. Additional functions provided by the different biomass systems are given in Table 2. The plants would not explicitly be built in order to provide these additional functions. Nev- ertheless they substitute processes which would otherwise be necessary to provide the additional functions if the plants did not exist (e.g. heat supply by conventional fossil fuels).

In this study the term "reference system" refers to all the processes needed for the substitution of the additional functions (e.g. conventional heat production or storage and application of undigested manure). The assumed reference systems are also given in Table 2.
As stated in the introduction, a practical problem of the avoided burdens approach is the selection of the substitutes

[4, 12-15, 23]. Information on the conventional disposal/ management of manure and organic residues used as sub- strates in the biomass-plants was obtained in personal interviews with plant operators.

For the co-product heat a fossil heat supply mix according to a study by the BMU, Germany [24] was assumed as the substitute (see "reference production heat"). According to this study, this heat supply mix rep- resents the additional fossil-based energy sources that would have to be used for heat generation if the biomass- based heat generation capacities were unavailable.
For the co-product nutrients according to the common agricultural practice conventional, synthetic N-fertilizer is assumed to be the substitute.
System Boundary

According to the ISO standards [10] the system boundary "defines the unit processes to be included in the system to be modeled". Figure 1 describes the life cycle stages considered within the system boundary. The unit processes of each life cycle stage are described in the Life Cycle Inventory, where it is also stated for which unit processes regional data has been collected and for which unit pro- cesses data from a database have been used.

Life cycle stages which cover direct life cycle emissions of electricity generation in biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of the study [7] and are shown in light collared boxes in Fig. 1. To cover indirect environmental impacts or avoided burdens from the replacement of the conventional production or provision of the additional functions the following life cycle stages have been intro- duced: "reference use land", "reference use manure",

Life Cycle Inventory

Regional data of 34 biomass plants all over Austria were collected in personal interviews with plant operators for anaerobic digestion plants by Laaber et al. [25] and for wood-chip-CHP plants by Siegl [26]. The questionnaire was elaborated in several meetings of plant operators, plant suppliers, planners and bioenergy research representatives. It covered specific process characterizing parameters in three fields:

<list>general functional description (e.g. quality and quantity of substrates, yields, transport distances and means of transportation, substrate dosage, plant type, digestate storage type (covered/open) digestate treatment and application technique, mode of ash disposal, product gas quantity and quality, energy conversion, thermal energy use, etc.) 
measurable process conditions [e.g. COD, TS, VSS, TKN, NH4-N of substrates and digestate, CH4, CO2, H2S, O2-concentration in biogas, flue gas emissions (CO, CH4, NMVOC, NOx, particulates, SO2), etc.] 
calculable variables (e.g. substrate input in t/year, especially for AD: residence time, hydraulic load, VSS degradation, digestate production in t/year, emissions</list>
from digestate, biogas/methane yield, calorific value, etc.).

For necessary additional calculations of emissions, emission factors and calculation methods are taken from literature, which are described in the following paragraphs. Global data on environmental impacts of transport services or the production of construction materials are taken from the Ecoinvent database [27], see Fig. 1.
Direct Life Cycle Emissions
Data collection, estimations and calculation methods for the calculation of direct life cycle emissions of the different types of biomass plants are described in detail in Part I of this study [7].
Reference Use Land (REF Land)

The need for the incorporation of land use change, caused by biomass production for bioenergy, into the LCA is demonstrated by different literature sources [1, 20-22] and is mentioned in the introduction. According to Bergsma et al. [16] the cultivation of biomass for energy purposes leads to the displacement of crop production as well as the displacement of land cover prior to biomass production for energy.

Data on the alternative land and biomass use (before the introduction of the biomass-systems) have been collected in personal interviews with plant operators.

As a result of the information gathered in the interviews and case studies from literature [6, 16, 21, 28] the reference uses given in Table 3 are assumed for the different types of biomass considered within this study. The assumed refer- ence use for the alternative land use is credited to the biomass system, the assumed reference use of the alter- native biomass use is charged to the biomass system. For reasons of simplicity and better presentability of the results both reference uses for land are combined to one life cycle stage, which can be positive if the effect of the alternative biomass use prevails or negative, if the effect of alternative land use prevails.

Changes in organic carbon stocks due to land use change are especially important for green house gas balances [19,21, 22]. Depending on the earlier use of the land and the crop to be established, the land use change can be a benefit or a disad- vantage depending for example on whether a forest is con- verted to agricultural land for bioenergy production (loss of carbon stocks) or set-aside land is taken into production (carbon stock may increase) [22].

The land use changes assumed for the biomass pro- duction in this study (Table 3) are: set aside land-crop land, crop land-crop land and forest-forest. As most set- aside land in Austria is grassland that is regularly mowed or ploughed [28] and on the other hand 20% of the land that is needed for biomass production for the AD-plants investigated is used for the production of grass-silage it is assumed that no change in organic carbon stocks occurs. If the type of land does not change with the land use change again no change in the organic carbon stocks is assumed. Furthermore because changes of carbon in soil are very site-specific and highly dependent on former and current agronomic practices, climate, and soil characteristics [19]
and there are often uncertainties in the levels of carbon stock changes due to variations in local conditions and lack of reliable field trial data [21].
Reference Use Manure (REF Manure)

By the introduction of anaerobic digestion plants, which use manure as a substrate, the emissions caused by the conventional management of manure (reference use) can be avoided. Therefore, a credit for the "reference use manure" is given to the biomass conversion system.

Data on the yearly amount and type of manure used in AD plants, as well as a possible processing, storage and spreading conditions before the introduction of the AD plant are collected in interviews with plant operators. From these amounts the energy input and emissions caused by the transport to the field and the spreading process (without gaseous emissions originating from the manure itself) are calculated using the corresponding ecoinvent datasets [27].

According to Amon et al. [30] manure management, consisting of the storage and spreading of manure, is a significant source of CH4, NH3 and N2O emissions to the atmosphere. Due to a lack of measurement data, gaseous emissions from manure management are calculated via emission factors derived from different literature sources (see Table 4).

Ammonia and dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the storage occur only from the surface of stored liquid manure [31]. Ammonia emissions are dependent on physical properties like the concentration of total ammoniacal Nitrogen (TAN), dry matter or pH-value as well as envi- ronmental conditions like temperature, wind speed or precipitations [30, 32]. Dinitrogen monoxide is formed under anoxic conditions in the crust (often formed on the surface of the stored manure) when ammonium is nitrified or during the denitrification of nitrate under anaerobic

conditions [33]. For the life cycle inventory they are cal- culated depending on the manure type (cattle or swine) and storage duration in proportion to the surface area of the open storage facilities. Methane emissions from the storage are calculated dependent on the amount of manure stored and the storage duration with different emission factors for cattle and swine manure. Methane emissions from the spreading of manure are very small in comparison to emissions from storage [30]. For the life cycle inventory they are calculated dependent on the amount of manure spread. According to Sommer and Hutchings [31] gaseous emissions from agriculture, and especially from the spreading of solid and liquid manure, are one of the major sources for atmospheric ammonia in Europe. Ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are influenced by a multitude of parameters like concentration of TAN, air temperature, pH-value, evaporation rate, crop canopy size and complexity, crust formation, surface roughness of field, wind speed, solar radiation, total area of manure applied, application technique and infiltration to soil [31]. For the

life cycle inventory ammonia emissions are calculated dependent on the type of manure in proportion to the concentration of TAN. The TAN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. As mentioned above, ammonia emissions from the spreading of manure are essentially dependent on the application technique used [31, 33, 36, 53]. If the application technique "trail hose" is used instead of the "splash plate method" ammonia emissions are assumed to be reduced by 40% [31, 33, 36, 53, 54] and for the application technique "injection (closed slot)" by 86% [33, 36, 45, 51, 53, 54].

Dinitrogen monoxide emissions from the spreading of manure are again influenced by many parameters like manuring method, soil characteristics (humidity, concen- tration of Nitrogen or humus) or climatic conditions [33]. For the calculation of the life cycle inventory the IPCC GPG [52] emission factor of 0.0196 (+0.0154) kg N2O (kg TKN) is used. The TKN-concentration was quoted by plant operators for the different types of manure. Accord- ing to [36, 45, 51] the closed slot injection application technique increases N2O emissions by a factor of 2.95 in comparison to the "splash plate method".
33: Another substrate used in the anaerobic digestion plants under study, are organic residues like organic household waste or food industry wastes which would be composted if the anaerobic digestion plants did not exist. Environmental bur- dens resulting from energy input as well as emissions, which occur during the conventional disposal route of composting (= reference use), are avoided by the introduction of anaerobic digestion plants. Therefore, a credit for the "reference use residues" is given to the biomass conversion system.

Composting is the purposeful aerobic biodegradation of organic matter (e.g. yard and food waste) by microorgan- isms (bacteria, yeasts and fungi). Gaseous degradation products are mainly carbon dioxide and (depending on the pH-value and nitrogen concentration) ammonia. On a micro scale, conditions often are shifting between aerobic and anaerobic, leading to anaerobic degradation products like methane and dinitrogen monoxide [55].
For the calculation of gaseous emission from the com- posting process for the life cycle inventory, emission fac- tors derived from various literature sources are used (see Table 5).
34: In all biomass plants investigated in the present study, beside the main product electricity, a certain amount of heat is pro- duced and a part of this is used in district heating systems. It is assumed, that by the utilization of this heat, the production of heat by conventional means (natural gas, oil, wood, etc.) is substituted. Therefore, a credit for the "reference production heat" is given to the biomass conversion system.

For the base-scenario a "heat supply mix" consisting of 52.9% natural gas, 41.5% oil, 1.5% coal and 4.1% elec- tricity is assumed. This distribution of fuels describes according to [24] the additional fossil-based energy sources that would have to be used for heat generation if the bio- mass-based heat generation capacities were unavailable.

The influence of the choice of fuel for the "reference production heat" on the total results is investigated in the sensitivity analysis. Life cycle inventory data for the dif- ferent heat production options is taken from the ecoinvent database [27].
35: In the case of anaerobic digestion plants the co-product digestate is used as fertilizer. The concentration of TKN in the digestates of the AD plants investigated ranges from 2.3 to 7.5 g/L and TAN-concentrations between 1.3 and 5.6 g/L were observed depending on the substrate-mix. Application rates between 10 and 75 m³ /ha were quoted by plant oper- ators depended on the TKN/TAN concentration of the dig- estate, the crop to be fertilized and the type of soil. Digestate application has the function of applying nutrients to the soil. The reference uses of the substrates (manure and compost application) fulfill the same function. Because N-emissions are different for the two scenarios, a nitrogen balance was calculated, in order to determine if the biomass-system or the reference-systems provide more nitrogen available for fertilization. Depending on the AD plant type both cases are possible. Similarly to the co-product heat, it is assumed that by the utilization of the surplus nitrogen, the production and application of N-fertilizer by conventional means is substi- tuted. Therefore, the energy inputs and emissions from the

38: In this paper the internationally well known mid-point method "CML 2001", developed by Guinée et al. [62] is applied. It covers all emission- and resource-related impacts, for which practical and acceptable characteriza- tion methods are available. The following impact catego- ries are used: climate change (GWP), acidification (AP), eutrophication (EP), creation of photochemical oxidants (= smog) (POCP), depletion of abiotic resources (ADP), depletion of the ozone-layer (ODP), land use (LUC) and different toxicity potentials (TP).
39: Operating Parameters of the Biomass Plants Investigated

Table 6 shows the mean values of the operating parameters of the different biomass plant types investigated. All AD plants investigated are of the CSTR (Completely Stirred Tank Reactor) type with at least 2 stages. The investigation shows that the electric power installed is smaller for anaerobic digestion plants (AD) than for combustion and gasification plants. The degree of heat utilization is higher for small anaerobic digestion plants than for other AD plant types. The highest degree of heat utilization is found for the gasification plant investigated.
40: The global warming potential describes emissions of dif- ferent greenhouse gases (most important for this study are CO2, CO, CH4 and N20) into the air expressed in kg CO2- equivalents.

The green house gas emissions avoided through the replacement of conventional reference systems are higher than the direct life cycle emissions for all types of biomass plants investigated, resulting in emission-savings. The extent of the saving depends mainly on the substrates used and the degree of heat utilization. Results are illus- trated in Fig. 2. For anaerobic digestion of mainly energy crops the smallest savings are calculated. For this plant type the reference production fertilizer is responsible for

approximately 50% of the total credits given. For the remaining anaerobic digestion plant types the reference use of manure and residues contributes the main part to the total avoided emissions. The highest savings are calculated for small AD plants <50 kWe1 due to their substrate mixture and the comparable high degree of heat utilization. Another explanation is the fact that the comparable low electrical efficiency causes higher credits per kWhel For combustion and gasification plants more than 97% of the avoided emissions originate from the reference production heat. Savings are higher for gasification due to the higher degree of heat utilization.

Emissions caused by the UCTE³ electricity production mix according to [27] are higher than the direct life cycle emissions from most of the biomass technologies. Only direct life cycle emissions from the plant types AD, PI and AD, <50 kW are at the same level like emissions from the UCTE production mix.
41: The global warming potential describes emissions of dif- ferent greenhouse gases (most important for this study are CO2, CO, CH4 and N20) into the air expressed in kg CO2- equivalents.

Emissions caused by the UCTE³ electricity production mix according to [27] are higher than the direct life cycle emissions from most of the biomass technologies. 42: For the AP the emissions of acidifying substances to the air (most important for this study: NHx, NO and SO2) are summed up expressed in SO2-equivalents. The EP covers emissions of nutrients to air, water and soil, which cause over-fertilization (most important for this study: NHx and NOX) expressed in PO4-equivalents. As the emission sources for both impact categories are very similar, they are discussed together. Results are illustrated in Fig. 2.

Ammonia emissions from the reference use of manure and organic residues (composting) contribute the main part to the total credits given, while emissions from the refer- ence production heat are very low. The consideration of avoided emissions leads to clear emission-savings for the plant types AD, R and AD, <50 kW. For the plant types AD, M and AD, PI the total result is approximately zero, which means that direct life cycle emissions caused by the production of 1 kWh electricity are as high as indirect emission savings by the reference systems. Compared to the UCTE production mix electricity generation in these plant types still leads to an emission reduction. For the plant types AD, EC; C, WC and G, WC emissions remain even after the consideration of the credits for conventional reference systems.

Concerning the acidification potential direct life cycle emissions of anaerobic digestion plants are by far higher than for the UCTE production mix and at the same level or a bit lower for gasification and combustion, respectively. After the consideration of the different reference systems

only the biomass plant type AD, EC shows higher emis- sions than the UCTE production mix. 46: This impact category covers photo-oxidant formation (better known as summer smog) by volatile organic com- pounds and CO expressed in kg ethylene (C2H4) equiva- lents. Results are illustrated in Fig. 2.

For anaerobic digestion plants the reference systems for heat, manure and organic residues contribute the main part of the total credits given. For the life cycle stages REF manure and REF residues methane emissions are respon- sible for the high POCP. Direct life cycle emissions are higher than the credits given for the reference systems, SO that emissions remain for all types of anaerobic digestion plants. The remaining emissions are lower than emissions from the UCTE production mix. The lowest emissions result for the AD plant type using a pilot injection gas engine due to the high credits given for the reference use of manure. For this plant type, methane emissions from the storage of the manure in open storage facilities are reduced in comparison to the emissions caused by the storage of the digestate, by the introduction of the biomass system due to a lower methane formation potential of the digestate and the construction of gas tight storage facilities.

For combustion and gasification of wood chips the consideration of the reference systems leads to emission- savings. Approximately 90% of the total credits originate from the reference production of heat.

ADP covers the use of fossil fuels and mineral resources; the reference substance is Antimony (Sb). For the ODP emissions of ozone-depleting gases to the air are summed up and expressed in trichlorofluoromethane (CFC 11) equivalents. As the emission sources (the use of fossil fuels) are very similar for both impact categories they are discussed together. Results are shown in Fig. 3.

With exception of the plant type AD, PI emission-sav- ings are calculated for all other biomass plant types. As mentioned in Part I of this paper, direct life cycle emissions of the plant type AD, PI are higher than for all other plant types due to the need for ignition oil in the electricity generation step. Emissions from the reference production heat are responsible for the main part of the total credits. The highest emission-savings are calculated for the plant types with the highest degree of heat utilization (AD, <50 kW and G, WC). For the plant type AD, EC the reference production of fertilizer contributes about 26% to the total credits. Depending on the composition of the substrates the reference use of organic residues contributes also a substantial part to the total credits given (e.g. 26% for the plant type AD, R).

In comparison to the UCTE electricity production mix total emissions and direct life cycle emissions are lower for all most all plant types and both impact categories. Only direct life cycle emissions from the plant type AD, PI in the impact category ODP are higher than emissions from the UCTE production mix.

direct life cycle emissions REF land
[62]. The unit of the indicator result is m² a-1 Results are shown in Fig. 3.

The reference system which contributes the main part to the total credits for AD plants is (depending on the share of manure/residues in the substrate mix) REF manure followed by REF residues. For combustion and gasification plants REF land contributes the main part to the total credits. In total land use remains even after the consideration of the reference systems for almost all plant types. Only for the plant type AD, R marginal savings are calculated. Toxicity Potentials (HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP)

The impact category human toxicity potential (HTP) COV- ers the impacts of toxic substances present in the envi- ronment on human health [62]. The ecotoxicity potentials cover the impacts of toxic substances emitted to air, water and soil on terrestrial (TAETP) and aquatic (freshwater: FAETP, marine: MAETP) ecosystems. The unit of the

Fig. 3 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass-plants for the impact categories ADP, ODP and LUC considering direct life cycle emissions (positive values) and credits for reference systems (negative values)
indicator result is kg 1,4 dichlorobenzene (1,4 DCB) equivalent. Results for the different types of plants are presented in Fig. 4.
The contribution of the different reference systems to the total credits is similar for each plant type in the dif- ferent impact categories covering toxicity.
The reference production heat is important for all plant types, contributing between 22 and 98% to the total credits depending on the degree of heat utilization and the impact category.

Depending on the share of organic residues on the substrate mix the reference use of residues is important for the plant types AD, R; AD, PI and AD, <50 kW, especially for the impact categories HTP and MAETP.
For the plant type AD, EC and to a lower extend AD, <50 kW the credit for the reference production of fertilizer is responsible for a main part (up to 68%) of the total credits.

In total for most of the plant types and impact categories emissions remain even after the consideration of reference systems. Emission-savings are only calculated for the plant type G, WC in the impact category HTP and AD, R and G, WC in the impact category MAETP. The remaining

emissions are lower than the emissions caused by the UCTE electricity production mix in most of the cases under study. Only in the impact category HTP emissions from the plant type AD, <50 kW and in the impact category FAETP emissions from the plant type AD, <50 kW; C, WC and G, WC are higher than emissions from the UCTE electricity production mix.
Sensitivity analysis measures the extent to which changes in the inventory results and characterization models affect the impact indicator results.
Sensitivity Analysis: REF Manure and REF Residues

The first sensitivity analysis covers the influence of uncertainties in the calculation of gaseous emissions (and energy input) from the handling of manure and organic residues (see Tables 4, 5) on the total results for AD plants. The calculation of gaseous emissions from the handling of manure and the handling of the co-product digestate (direct

Fig. 4 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass-plants for the toxicity potentials HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP considering direct life cycle emissions (positive values) and credits for reference systems (negative values)

life cycle emissions) is based on the same assumptions and calculation models (see Part I [7]). Therefore, uncertainties of total results of direct life cycle emissions caused by uncertainties from the calculation of gaseous emissions from the handling of digestate are considered in this sen- sitivity analysis. Results of this sensitivity analysis are illustrated in Fig. 5.

While direct life cycle emissions are influenced in this sensitivity analysis to the greatest extend in the impact cat- egories AP and EP (Part I [7]) the variation of total results in these impact categories is kind of compensated by the uncertainties of gaseous emissions in the "reference system

manure". For the plant types AD, M and AD, PI total results in these impact categories could shift from minimal emis- sion-savings to emissions which are then at the same level like emissions from the UCTE electricity production mix.

The highest deviation of total results is observed in the impact categories GWP, POCP and MAETP especially for plant types using a high share of organic residues in the substrate mix. This can be explained by the high uncertainty in the calculation of dinitrogen monoxide (GWP) and methane emissions (GWP and POCP) as well as energy input (MAETP) from/to the composting process (see Table 5). For the plant type AD, PI the high deviation could shift the total

Fig. 5 Sensitivity analysis: influence of uncertainties in the calculation of gaseous emissions (and energy input) from the handling of digestate, manure and organic residues on the total results for AD plants

results in the impact category POCP from being the least polluting option of all AD plants, either to being one of the most polluting options (at the level of emissions from the UCTE electricity production mix), or to being the least polluting option of all plant types. In the impact category MAETP total results could shift from emission-savings to emissions for the plant type AD, R, on the other side emis- sions could shift to emission-savings for the plant type AD, <50 kW.

The sensitivity analysis "reference production heat" shows the influence of the choice of fuel for the heat production in the reference system (100% of natural gas, coal or wood) on the total results. For the base scenario a heat supply mix consisting of 52.9% natural gas, 41.5% oil, 1.5% coal and 4.1% electricity according to [24] is assumed. The sensi- tivity analysis for the plant type G, WC, which shows the

highest degree of heat utilization and therefore, the highest alternation of total results in this sensitivity analysis is shown in Fig. 6. Results are shown in % of the base sce- nario (base scenario = 100%, white boxes) for all impact categories and 3 different heat supply fuels.

As the heat supply mix in the base scenario consists to 52.9% of natural gas, alternations of the total results in the scenario "100% natural gas" are comparable low in most of the impact categories. In general LCIA results (envi- ronmental burdens) would be higher in all impact catego- ries than in the base scenario. In the impact categories AP, POCP, TAETP and MAETP they would be nearly twice as high (in the case of POCP and MAETP shifting from emission-savings to emissions). This result can be explained by the fact that emissions from the heat supply by natural gas are lower than from the heat supply mix (base scenario).

If the fuel for the reference production heat was 100% coal, LCIA results (environmental burdens) would be reduced. Emission savings would be increased in the impact categories GWP, POCP, ADP, HTP and MAETP. Emissions would be reduced in the impact category EP. Emissions would shift to emission-savings in the impact categories AP, TAETP and FAETP. Only in the impact category ODP emission-savings would be reduced to approximately zero.

If the fuel for the reference production heat was 100% wood, the calculated emission profile would be quite dif- ferent: in the impact categories GWP, POCP, ADP, ODP and MAETP emission-savings from the base scenario would turn into low emissions. Emissions in the impact categories AP, EP, LUC and FAETP would be reduced and in the impact category TAETP shifted into emission-sav- ings. Emission-savings in the impact category HTP would be increased.
Ranking of Different Biomass Plant Types

A ranking was calculated for the simple evaluation and comparison of overall environmental performance without any weighing of the importance of the different impact categories: within every impact category the various alter- natives are ranked according to the result. For each alter- native a score is calculated by adding the values of the ranks of all 11 impact categories. In the total ranking the biomass plant type with the lowest environmental burden takes the 1st place and the one with the highest environmental burden the 7th place. The result of the ranking is shown in Table 7.

Considering direct life cycle emissions, as well as credits for various reference systems, the anaerobic digestion plant types using mainly organic residues (AD, R) shows the best environmental performance. Following

are the biomass plant type employing gasification of wood chips (G, WC), the small anaerobic digestion plant type (AD, <50 kW) and the plant type employing combustion of wood chips (C, WC). On the 5th place is the anaerobic digestion plant type using mainly manure (AD, M). At the end of the ranking the anaerobic digestion plant types using mainly energy crops (AD, EC) and using a pilot injection gas engine (AD, PI) are found.

The order of plant types is very different from the order in the ranking considering only direct life cycle emissions (see Part I [7] and Table 7). The reference systems with the strongest influence on the order of the ranking are REF heat and REF residues. The plant type AD, <50 kW, which shows the highest degree of heat utilization (of AD plants), and the plant type AD, R with a share of 69% of residues in the substrate mix, move forward 4 places in the ranking each. The plant type AD, EC which shows the lowest degree of heat utilization and no other credits for reference systems of manure or residues, falls behind 5 places.

Because a high influence of the degree of heat utilization on the total results and the order of the ranking could be identified, an optimization scenario assuming 30% heat utilization for all plant types is calculated (Table 7). This scenario allows a comparison of the different plant types without the "distortion" of different degrees of heat utili- zation. The order of plant types is again very different from

the rankings presented before. In this scenario the biomass plant type employing combustion of wood chips shows the best environmental performance, followed by AD, R; AD, M and G, WC. The plant types AD, EC; AD, <50 kW and AD, PI are found at the end of the ranking.

As the calculations are based on existing plants, the possibilities of heat utilization seem to be dependent on the location of the plant, which is characteristic for each plant type. Never the less this scenario shows the high potential of improvement of the overall environmental performance by increasing the degree of heat utilization.
