UCTE electricity production mix. The highest land use is calculated for small AD plants and the gasification plant.
Toxicity Potentials (HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP)

The impact category human toxicity potential (HTP) COV- ers the impacts of toxic substances present in the envi- ronment on human health [62]. The ecotoxicity potentials cover the impacts of toxic substances emitted to air, water and soil on terrestrial (TAETP) and aquatic (freshwater: FAETP, marine: MAETP) ecosystems. The unit of the

Fig. 3 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass-plants for the impact categories ADP, ODP and LUC considering direct life cycle emissions (positive values) and credits for reference systems (negative values)
indicator result is kg 1,4 dichlorobenzene (1,4 DCB) equivalent. Results for the different types of plants are presented in Fig. 4.
The contribution of the different reference systems to the total credits is similar for each plant type in the dif- ferent impact categories covering toxicity.
The reference production heat is important for all plant types, contributing between 22 and 98% to the total credits depending on the degree of heat utilization and the impact category.

Depending on the share of organic residues on the substrate mix the reference use of residues is important for the plant types AD, R; AD, PI and AD, <50 kW, especially for the impact categories HTP and MAETP.
For the plant type AD, EC and to a lower extend AD, <50 kW the credit for the reference production of fertilizer is responsible for a main part (up to 68%) of the total credits.

In total for most of the plant types and impact categories emissions remain even after the consideration of reference systems. Emission-savings are only calculated for the plant type G, WC in the impact category HTP and AD, R and G, WC in the impact category MAETP. The remaining

emissions are lower than the emissions caused by the UCTE electricity production mix in most of the cases under study. Only in the impact category HTP emissions from the plant type AD, <50 kW and in the impact category FAETP emissions from the plant type AD, <50 kW; C, WC and G, WC are higher than emissions from the UCTE electricity production mix.
Sensitivity analysis measures the extent to which changes in the inventory results and characterization models affect the impact indicator results.
Sensitivity Analysis: REF Manure and REF Residues

The first sensitivity analysis covers the influence of uncertainties in the calculation of gaseous emissions (and energy input) from the handling of manure and organic residues (see Tables 4, 5) on the total results for AD plants. The calculation of gaseous emissions from the handling of manure and the handling of the co-product digestate (direct

Fig. 4 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass-plants for the toxicity potentials HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP considering direct life cycle emissions (positive values) and credits for reference systems (negative values)

life cycle emissions) is based on the same assumptions and calculation models (see Part I [7]). Therefore, uncertainties of total results of direct life cycle emissions caused by uncertainties from the calculation of gaseous emissions from the handling of digestate are considered in this sen- sitivity analysis. Results of this sensitivity analysis are illustrated in Fig. 5.

While direct life cycle emissions are influenced in this sensitivity analysis to the greatest extend in the impact cat- egories AP and EP (Part I [7]) the variation of total results in these impact categories is kind of compensated by the uncertainties of gaseous emissions in the "reference system

manure". For the plant types AD, M and AD, PI total results in these impact categories could shift from minimal emis- sion-savings to emissions which are then at the same level like emissions from the UCTE electricity production mix.

The highest deviation of total results is observed in the impact categories GWP, POCP and MAETP especially for plant types using a high share of organic residues in the substrate mix. This can be explained by the high uncertainty in the calculation of dinitrogen monoxide (GWP) and methane emissions (GWP and POCP) as well as energy input (MAETP) from/to the composting process (see Table 5). For the plant type AD, PI the high deviation could shift the total

Fig. 5 Sensitivity analysis: influence of uncertainties in the calculation of gaseous emissions (and energy input) from the handling of digestate, manure and organic residues on the total results for AD plants

results in the impact category POCP from being the least polluting option of all AD plants, either to being one of the most polluting options (at the level of emissions from the UCTE electricity production mix), or to being the least polluting option of all plant types. In the impact category MAETP total results could shift from emission-savings to emissions for the plant type AD, R, on the other side emis- sions could shift to emission-savings for the plant type AD, <50 kW.

The sensitivity analysis "reference production heat" shows the influence of the choice of fuel for the heat production in the reference system (100% of natural gas, coal or wood) on the total results. For the base scenario a heat supply mix consisting of 52.9% natural gas, 41.5% oil, 1.5% coal and 4.1% electricity according to [24] is assumed. The sensi- tivity analysis for the plant type G, WC, which shows the

highest degree of heat utilization and therefore, the highest alternation of total results in this sensitivity analysis is shown in Fig. 6. Results are shown in % of the base sce- nario (base scenario = 100%, white boxes) for all impact categories and 3 different heat supply fuels.

As the heat supply mix in the base scenario consists to 52.9% of natural gas, alternations of the total results in the scenario "100% natural gas" are comparable low in most of the impact categories. In general LCIA results (envi- ronmental burdens) would be higher in all impact catego- ries than in the base scenario. In the impact categories AP, POCP, TAETP and MAETP they would be nearly twice as high (in the case of POCP and MAETP shifting from emission-savings to emissions). This result can be explained by the fact that emissions from the heat supply by natural gas are lower than from the heat supply mix (base scenario).

If the fuel for the reference production heat was 100% coal, LCIA results (environmental burdens) would be reduced. Emission savings would be increased in the impact categories GWP, POCP, ADP, HTP and MAETP. Emissions would be reduced in the impact category EP. Emissions would shift to emission-savings in the impact categories AP, TAETP and FAETP. Only in the impact category ODP emission-savings would be reduced to approximately zero.

If the fuel for the reference production heat was 100% wood, the calculated emission profile would be quite dif- ferent: in the impact categories GWP, POCP, ADP, ODP and MAETP emission-savings from the base scenario would turn into low emissions. Emissions in the impact categories AP, EP, LUC and FAETP would be reduced and in the impact category TAETP shifted into emission-sav- ings. Emission-savings in the impact category HTP would be increased.

Emissions would shift to emission-savings in the impact categories AP, TAETP and FAETP. Only in the impact category ODP emission-savings would be reduced to approximately zero.

If the fuel for the reference production heat was 100% wood, the calculated emission profile would be quite dif- ferent: in the impact categories GWP, POCP, ADP, ODP and MAETP emission-savings from the base scenario would turn into low emissions. Emissions in the impact categories AP, EP, LUC and FAETP would be reduced and in the impact category TAETP shifted into emission-sav- ings. Emission-savings in the impact category HTP would be increased.
Ranking of Different Biomass Plant Types

A ranking was calculated for the simple evaluation and comparison of overall environmental performance without any weighing of the importance of the different impact categories: within every impact category the various alter- natives are ranked according to the result. For each alter- native a score is calculated by adding the values of the ranks of all 11 impact categories. In the total ranking the biomass plant type with the lowest environmental burden takes the 1st place and the one with the highest environmental burden the 7th place. The result of the ranking is shown in Table 7.

Considering direct life cycle emissions, as well as credits for various reference systems, the anaerobic digestion plant types using mainly organic residues (AD, R) shows the best environmental performance. Following

are the biomass plant type employing gasification of wood chips (G, WC), the small anaerobic digestion plant type (AD, <50 kW) and the plant type employing combustion of wood chips (C, WC). On the 5th place is the anaerobic digestion plant type using mainly manure (AD, M). At the end of the ranking the anaerobic digestion plant types using mainly energy crops (AD, EC) and using a pilot injection gas engine (AD, PI) are found.

The order of plant types is very different from the order in the ranking considering only direct life cycle emissions (see Part I [7] and Table 7). The reference systems with the strongest influence on the order of the ranking are REF heat and REF residues. The plant type AD, <50 kW, which shows the highest degree of heat utilization (of AD plants), and the plant type AD, R with a share of 69% of residues in the substrate mix, move forward 4 places in the ranking each. The plant type AD, EC which shows the lowest degree of heat utilization and no other credits for reference systems of manure or residues, falls behind 5 places.

Because a high influence of the degree of heat utilization on the total results and the order of the ranking could be identified, an optimization scenario assuming 30% heat utilization for all plant types is calculated (Table 7). This scenario allows a comparison of the different plant types without the "distortion" of different degrees of heat utili- zation. The order of plant types is again very different from

the rankings presented before. In this scenario the biomass plant type employing combustion of wood chips shows the best environmental performance, followed by AD, R; AD, M and G, WC. The plant types AD, EC; AD, <50 kW and AD, PI are found at the end of the ranking.

As the calculations are based on existing plants, the possibilities of heat utilization seem to be dependent on the location of the plant, which is characteristic for each plant type. Never the less this scenario shows the high potential of improvement of the overall environmental performance by increasing the degree of heat utilization.
62: This impact category covers photo-oxidant formation (better known as summer smog) by volatile organic com- pounds and CO expressed in kg ethylene (C2H4) equiva- lents. Results are illustrated in Fig. 2.

For anaerobic digestion plants the reference systems for heat, manure and organic residues contribute the main part of the total credits given. For the life cycle stages REF manure and REF residues methane emissions are respon- sible for the high POCP. Direct life cycle emissions are higher than the credits given for the reference systems, SO that emissions remain for all types of anaerobic digestion plants. The remaining emissions are lower than emissions from the UCTE production mix. The lowest emissions result for the AD plant type using a pilot injection gas engine due to the high credits given for the reference use of manure. For this plant type, methane emissions from the storage of the manure in open storage facilities are reduced in comparison to the emissions caused by the storage of the digestate, by the introduction of the biomass system due to a lower methane formation potential of the digestate and the construction of gas tight storage facilities.

For combustion and gasification of wood chips the consideration of the reference systems leads to emission- savings. Approximately 90% of the total credits originate from the reference production of heat.

ADP covers the use of fossil fuels and mineral resources; the reference substance is Antimony (Sb). For the ODP emissions of ozone-depleting gases to the air are summed up and expressed in trichlorofluoromethane (CFC 11) equivalents. As the emission sources (the use of fossil fuels) are very similar for both impact categories they are discussed together. Results are shown in Fig. 3.

With exception of the plant type AD, PI emission-sav- ings are calculated for all other biomass plant types. As mentioned in Part I of this paper, direct life cycle emissions of the plant type AD, PI are higher than for all other plant types due to the need for ignition oil in the electricity generation step. Emissions from the reference production heat are responsible for the main part of the total credits. The highest emission-savings are calculated for the plant types with the highest degree of heat utilization (AD, <50 kW and G, WC). For the plant type AD, EC the reference production of fertilizer contributes about 26% to the total credits. Depending on the composition of the substrates the reference use of organic residues contributes also a substantial part to the total credits given (e.g. 26% for the plant type AD, R).

In comparison to the UCTE electricity production mix total emissions and direct life cycle emissions are lower for all most all plant types and both impact categories. Only direct life cycle emissions from the plant type AD, PI in the impact category ODP are higher than emissions from the UCTE production mix.
This impact category deals with the loss of land as a resource, in the sense of being temporarily unavailable

direct life cycle emissions REF land
[62]. The unit of the indicator result is m² a-1 Results are shown in Fig. 3.

The reference system which contributes the main part to the total credits for AD plants is (depending on the share of manure/residues in the substrate mix) REF manure followed by REF residues. For combustion and gasification plants REF land contributes the main part to the total credits. In total land use remains even after the consideration of the reference systems for almost all plant types. Only for the plant type AD, R marginal savings are calculated. For the remaining plant types the land use is higher than for the
UCTE electricity production mix. The highest land use is calculated for small AD plants and the gasification plant.
Toxicity Potentials (HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP)

The impact category human toxicity potential (HTP) COV- ers the impacts of toxic substances present in the envi- ronment on human health [62]. The ecotoxicity potentials cover the impacts of toxic substances emitted to air, water and soil on terrestrial (TAETP) and aquatic (freshwater: FAETP, marine: MAETP) ecosystems. The unit of the

Fig. 3 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass-plants for the impact categories ADP, ODP and LUC considering direct life cycle emissions (positive values) and credits for reference systems (negative values)
indicator result is kg 1,4 dichlorobenzene (1,4 DCB) equivalent. Results for the different types of plants are presented in Fig. 4.
The contribution of the different reference systems to the total credits is similar for each plant type in the dif- ferent impact categories covering toxicity.
The reference production heat is important for all plant types, contributing between 22 and 98% to the total credits depending on the degree of heat utilization and the impact category.

Depending on the share of organic residues on the substrate mix the reference use of residues is important for the plant types AD, R; AD, PI and AD, <50 kW, especially for the impact categories HTP and MAETP.
For the plant type AD, EC and to a lower extend AD, <50 kW the credit for the reference production of fertilizer is responsible for a main part (up to 68%) of the total credits.

In total for most of the plant types and impact categories emissions remain even after the consideration of reference systems. Emission-savings are only calculated for the plant type G, WC in the impact category HTP and AD, R and G, WC in the impact category MAETP. The remaining

emissions are lower than the emissions caused by the UCTE electricity production mix in most of the cases under study. Only in the impact category HTP emissions from the plant type AD, <50 kW and in the impact category FAETP emissions from the plant type AD, <50 kW; C, WC and G, WC are higher than emissions from the UCTE electricity production mix.
Sensitivity analysis measures the extent to which changes in the inventory results and characterization models affect the impact indicator results.
Sensitivity Analysis: REF Manure and REF Residues

The first sensitivity analysis covers the influence of uncertainties in the calculation of gaseous emissions (and energy input) from the handling of manure and organic residues (see Tables 4, 5) on the total results for AD plants. The calculation of gaseous emissions from the handling of manure and the handling of the co-product digestate (direct

Fig. 4 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass-plants for the toxicity potentials HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP considering direct life cycle emissions (positive values) and credits for reference systems (negative values)

life cycle emissions) is based on the same assumptions and calculation models (see Part I [7]). Therefore, uncertainties of total results of direct life cycle emissions caused by uncertainties from the calculation of gaseous emissions from the handling of digestate are considered in this sen- sitivity analysis. Results of this sensitivity analysis are illustrated in Fig. 5.

While direct life cycle emissions are influenced in this sensitivity analysis to the greatest extend in the impact cat- egories AP and EP (Part I [7]) the variation of total results in these impact categories is kind of compensated by the uncertainties of gaseous emissions in the "reference system

manure". For the plant types AD, M and AD, PI total results in these impact categories could shift from minimal emis- sion-savings to emissions which are then at the same level like emissions from the UCTE electricity production mix.

The highest deviation of total results is observed in the impact categories GWP, POCP and MAETP especially for plant types using a high share of organic residues in the substrate mix. This can be explained by the high uncertainty in the calculation of dinitrogen monoxide (GWP) and methane emissions (GWP and POCP) as well as energy input (MAETP) from/to the composting process (see Table 5). For the plant type AD, PI the high deviation could shift the total

Fig. 5 Sensitivity analysis: influence of uncertainties in the calculation of gaseous emissions (and energy input) from the handling of digestate, manure and organic residues on the total results for AD plants

results in the impact category POCP from being the least polluting option of all AD plants, either to being one of the most polluting options (at the level of emissions from the UCTE electricity production mix), or to being the least polluting option of all plant types. In the impact category MAETP total results could shift from emission-savings to emissions for the plant type AD, R, on the other side emis- sions could shift to emission-savings for the plant type AD, <50 kW.

The sensitivity analysis "reference production heat" shows the influence of the choice of fuel for the heat production in the reference system (100% of natural gas, coal or wood) on the total results. For the base scenario a heat supply mix consisting of 52.9% natural gas, 41.5% oil, 1.5% coal and 4.1% electricity according to [24] is assumed. The sensi- tivity analysis for the plant type G, WC, which shows the

highest degree of heat utilization and therefore, the highest alternation of total results in this sensitivity analysis is shown in Fig. 6. Results are shown in % of the base sce- nario (base scenario = 100%, white boxes) for all impact categories and 3 different heat supply fuels.

As the heat supply mix in the base scenario consists to 52.9% of natural gas, alternations of the total results in the scenario "100% natural gas" are comparable low in most of the impact categories. In general LCIA results (envi- ronmental burdens) would be higher in all impact catego- ries than in the base scenario. In the impact categories AP, POCP, TAETP and MAETP they would be nearly twice as high (in the case of POCP and MAETP shifting from emission-savings to emissions). This result can be explained by the fact that emissions from the heat supply by natural gas are lower than from the heat supply mix (base scenario).

If the fuel for the reference production heat was 100% coal, LCIA results (environmental burdens) would be reduced. Emission savings would be increased in the impact categories GWP, POCP, ADP, HTP and MAETP. Emissions would be reduced in the impact category EP. Emissions would shift to emission-savings in the impact categories AP, TAETP and FAETP. Only in the impact category ODP emission-savings would be reduced to approximately zero.

If the fuel for the reference production heat was 100% wood, the calculated emission profile would be quite dif- ferent: in the impact categories GWP, POCP, ADP, ODP and MAETP emission-savings from the base scenario would turn into low emissions. Emissions in the impact categories AP, EP, LUC and FAETP would be reduced and in the impact category TAETP shifted into emission-sav- ings. Emission-savings in the impact category HTP would be increased.

Emissions in the impact categories AP, EP, LUC and FAETP would be reduced and in the impact category TAETP shifted into emission-sav- ings. Emission-savings in the impact category HTP would be increased.
Ranking of Different Biomass Plant Types

A ranking was calculated for the simple evaluation and comparison of overall environmental performance without any weighing of the importance of the different impact categories: within every impact category the various alter- natives are ranked according to the result. For each alter- native a score is calculated by adding the values of the ranks of all 11 impact categories. In the total ranking the biomass plant type with the lowest environmental burden takes the 1st place and the one with the highest environmental burden the 7th place. The result of the ranking is shown in Table 7.

Considering direct life cycle emissions, as well as credits for various reference systems, the anaerobic digestion plant types using mainly organic residues (AD, R) shows the best environmental performance. Following

are the biomass plant type employing gasification of wood chips (G, WC), the small anaerobic digestion plant type (AD, <50 kW) and the plant type employing combustion of wood chips (C, WC). The plant type AD, EC which shows the lowest degree of heat utilization and no other credits for reference systems of manure or residues, falls behind 5 places.

Because a high influence of the degree of heat utilization on the total results and the order of the ranking could be identified, an optimization scenario assuming 30% heat utilization for all plant types is calculated (Table 7). the rankings presented before. In this scenario the biomass plant type employing combustion of wood chips shows the best environmental performance, followed by AD, R; AD, M and G, WC. The plant types AD, EC; AD, <50 kW and AD, PI are found at the end of the ranking.

Never the less this scenario shows the high potential of improvement of the overall environmental performance by increasing the degree of heat utilization.
64: This impact category covers photo-oxidant formation (better known as summer smog) by volatile organic com- pounds and CO expressed in kg ethylene (C2H4) equiva- lents. Results are illustrated in Fig. 2.

For anaerobic digestion plants the reference systems for heat, manure and organic residues contribute the main part of the total credits given. For the life cycle stages REF manure and REF residues methane emissions are respon- sible for the high POCP. Direct life cycle emissions are higher than the credits given for the reference systems, SO that emissions remain for all types of anaerobic digestion plants. The remaining emissions are lower than emissions from the UCTE production mix. The lowest emissions result for the AD plant type using a pilot injection gas engine due to the high credits given for the reference use of manure. For this plant type, methane emissions from the storage of the manure in open storage facilities are reduced in comparison to the emissions caused by the storage of the digestate, by the introduction of the biomass system due to a lower methane formation potential of the digestate and the construction of gas tight storage facilities.

For combustion and gasification of wood chips the consideration of the reference systems leads to emission- savings. Approximately 90% of the total credits originate from the reference production of heat.

ADP covers the use of fossil fuels and mineral resources; the reference substance is Antimony (Sb). For the ODP emissions of ozone-depleting gases to the air are summed up and expressed in trichlorofluoromethane (CFC 11) equivalents. As the emission sources (the use of fossil fuels) are very similar for both impact categories they are discussed together. Results are shown in Fig. 3.

With exception of the plant type AD, PI emission-sav- ings are calculated for all other biomass plant types. As mentioned in Part I of this paper, direct life cycle emissions of the plant type AD, PI are higher than for all other plant types due to the need for ignition oil in the electricity generation step. Emissions from the reference production heat are responsible for the main part of the total credits. The highest emission-savings are calculated for the plant types with the highest degree of heat utilization (AD, <50 kW and G, WC). For the plant type AD, EC the reference production of fertilizer contributes about 26% to the total credits. Depending on the composition of the substrates the reference use of organic residues contributes also a substantial part to the total credits given (e.g. 26% for the plant type AD, R).

In comparison to the UCTE electricity production mix total emissions and direct life cycle emissions are lower for all most all plant types and both impact categories. Only direct life cycle emissions from the plant type AD, PI in the impact category ODP are higher than emissions from the UCTE production mix.

The reference system which contributes the main part to the total credits for AD plants is (depending on the share of manure/residues in the substrate mix) REF manure followed by REF residues. For combustion and gasification plants REF land contributes the main part to the total credits. Toxicity Potentials (HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP)

The impact category human toxicity potential (HTP) COV- ers the impacts of toxic substances present in the envi- ronment on human health [62]. The ecotoxicity potentials cover the impacts of toxic substances emitted to air, water and soil on terrestrial (TAETP) and aquatic (freshwater: FAETP, marine: MAETP) ecosystems. The unit of the

Fig. 3 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass-plants for the impact categories ADP, ODP and LUC considering direct life cycle emissions (positive values) and credits for reference systems (negative values)
indicator result is kg 1,4 dichlorobenzene (1,4 DCB) equivalent. Results for the different types of plants are presented in Fig. 4.
The contribution of the different reference systems to the total credits is similar for each plant type in the dif- ferent impact categories covering toxicity.
The reference production heat is important for all plant types, contributing between 22 and 98% to the total credits depending on the degree of heat utilization and the impact category.

Depending on the share of organic residues on the substrate mix the reference use of residues is important for the plant types AD, R; AD, PI and AD, <50 kW, especially for the impact categories HTP and MAETP.
For the plant type AD, EC and to a lower extend AD, <50 kW the credit for the reference production of fertilizer is responsible for a main part (up to 68%) of the total credits.

In total for most of the plant types and impact categories emissions remain even after the consideration of reference systems. Emission-savings are only calculated for the plant type G, WC in the impact category HTP and AD, R and G, WC in the impact category MAETP. The remaining

emissions are lower than the emissions caused by the UCTE electricity production mix in most of the cases under study. Only in the impact category HTP emissions from the plant type AD, <50 kW and in the impact category FAETP emissions from the plant type AD, <50 kW; C, WC and G, WC are higher than emissions from the UCTE electricity production mix.
Sensitivity analysis measures the extent to which changes in the inventory results and characterization models affect the impact indicator results.
Sensitivity Analysis: REF Manure and REF Residues

The first sensitivity analysis covers the influence of uncertainties in the calculation of gaseous emissions (and energy input) from the handling of manure and organic residues (see Tables 4, 5) on the total results for AD plants. The calculation of gaseous emissions from the handling of manure and the handling of the co-product digestate (direct

Fig. 4 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass-plants for the toxicity potentials HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP considering direct life cycle emissions (positive values) and credits for reference systems (negative values)

life cycle emissions) is based on the same assumptions and calculation models (see Part I [7]). Therefore, uncertainties of total results of direct life cycle emissions caused by uncertainties from the calculation of gaseous emissions from the handling of digestate are considered in this sen- sitivity analysis. Results of this sensitivity analysis are illustrated in Fig. 5.

While direct life cycle emissions are influenced in this sensitivity analysis to the greatest extend in the impact cat- egories AP and EP (Part I [7]) the variation of total results in these impact categories is kind of compensated by the uncertainties of gaseous emissions in the "reference system

manure". For the plant types AD, M and AD, PI total results in these impact categories could shift from minimal emis- sion-savings to emissions which are then at the same level like emissions from the UCTE electricity production mix.

The highest deviation of total results is observed in the impact categories GWP, POCP and MAETP especially for plant types using a high share of organic residues in the substrate mix. This can be explained by the high uncertainty in the calculation of dinitrogen monoxide (GWP) and methane emissions (GWP and POCP) as well as energy input (MAETP) from/to the composting process (see Table 5). For the plant type AD, PI the high deviation could shift the total

Fig. 5 Sensitivity analysis: influence of uncertainties in the calculation of gaseous emissions (and energy input) from the handling of digestate, manure and organic residues on the total results for AD plants

results in the impact category POCP from being the least polluting option of all AD plants, either to being one of the most polluting options (at the level of emissions from the UCTE electricity production mix), or to being the least polluting option of all plant types. In the impact category MAETP total results could shift from emission-savings to emissions for the plant type AD, R, on the other side emis- sions could shift to emission-savings for the plant type AD, <50 kW.

The sensitivity analysis "reference production heat" shows the influence of the choice of fuel for the heat production in the reference system (100% of natural gas, coal or wood) on the total results. For the base scenario a heat supply mix consisting of 52.9% natural gas, 41.5% oil, 1.5% coal and 4.1% electricity according to [24] is assumed. The sensi- tivity analysis for the plant type G, WC, which shows the

highest degree of heat utilization and therefore, the highest alternation of total results in this sensitivity analysis is shown in Fig. 6. Results are shown in % of the base sce- nario (base scenario = 100%, white boxes) for all impact categories and 3 different heat supply fuels.

As the heat supply mix in the base scenario consists to 52.9% of natural gas, alternations of the total results in the scenario "100% natural gas" are comparable low in most of the impact categories. In general LCIA results (envi- ronmental burdens) would be higher in all impact catego- ries than in the base scenario. In the impact categories AP, POCP, TAETP and MAETP they would be nearly twice as high (in the case of POCP and MAETP shifting from emission-savings to emissions). This result can be explained by the fact that emissions from the heat supply by natural gas are lower than from the heat supply mix (base scenario).

If the fuel for the reference production heat was 100% coal, LCIA results (environmental burdens) would be reduced. Emission savings would be increased in the impact categories GWP, POCP, ADP, HTP and MAETP. Emissions would be reduced in the impact category EP. Emissions would shift to emission-savings in the impact categories AP, TAETP and FAETP. Only in the impact category ODP emission-savings would be reduced to approximately zero.

If the fuel for the reference production heat was 100% wood, the calculated emission profile would be quite dif- ferent: in the impact categories GWP, POCP, ADP, ODP and MAETP emission-savings from the base scenario would turn into low emissions. Emissions in the impact categories AP, EP, LUC and FAETP would be reduced and in the impact category TAETP shifted into emission-sav- ings. Emission-savings in the impact category HTP would be increased.

A ranking was calculated for the simple evaluation and comparison of overall environmental performance without any weighing of the importance of the different impact categories: within every impact category the various alter- natives are ranked according to the result. For each alter- native a score is calculated by adding the values of the ranks of all 11 impact categories. In the total ranking the biomass plant type with the lowest environmental burden takes the 1st place and the one with the highest environmental burden the 7th place. The result of the ranking is shown in Table 7.

Considering direct life cycle emissions, as well as credits for various reference systems, the anaerobic digestion plant types using mainly organic residues (AD, R) shows the best environmental performance. Following

are the biomass plant type employing gasification of wood chips (G, WC), the small anaerobic digestion plant type (AD, <50 kW) and the plant type employing combustion of wood chips (C, WC). At the end of the ranking the anaerobic digestion plant types using mainly energy crops (AD, EC) and using a pilot injection gas engine (AD, PI) are found.

The order of plant types is very different from the order in the ranking considering only direct life cycle emissions (see Part I [7] and Table 7). The reference systems with the strongest influence on the order of the ranking are REF heat and REF residues. The plant type AD, <50 kW, which shows the highest degree of heat utilization (of AD plants), and the plant type AD, R with a share of 69% of residues in the substrate mix, move forward 4 places in the ranking each. The plant type AD, EC which shows the lowest degree of heat utilization and no other credits for reference systems of manure or residues, falls behind 5 places.

Because a high influence of the degree of heat utilization on the total results and the order of the ranking could be identified, an optimization scenario assuming 30% heat utilization for all plant types is calculated (Table 7). This scenario allows a comparison of the different plant types without the "distortion" of different degrees of heat utili- zation. The order of plant types is again very different from

the rankings presented before. In this scenario the biomass plant type employing combustion of wood chips shows the best environmental performance, followed by AD, R; AD, M and G, WC. The plant types AD, EC; AD, <50 kW and AD, PI are found at the end of the ranking.

As the calculations are based on existing plants, the possibilities of heat utilization seem to be dependent on the location of the plant, which is characteristic for each plant type. Never the less this scenario shows the high potential of improvement of the overall environmental performance by increasing the degree of heat utilization.
66: This impact category covers photo-oxidant formation (better known as summer smog) by volatile organic com- pounds and CO expressed in kg ethylene (C2H4) equiva- lents. Results are illustrated in Fig. 2.

For anaerobic digestion plants the reference systems for heat, manure and organic residues contribute the main part of the total credits given. For the life cycle stages REF manure and REF residues methane emissions are respon- sible for the high POCP. Direct life cycle emissions are higher than the credits given for the reference systems, SO that emissions remain for all types of anaerobic digestion plants. The remaining emissions are lower than emissions from the UCTE production mix. The lowest emissions result for the AD plant type using a pilot injection gas engine due to the high credits given for the reference use of manure. For this plant type, methane emissions from the storage of the manure in open storage facilities are reduced in comparison to the emissions caused by the storage of the digestate, by the introduction of the biomass system due to a lower methane formation potential of the digestate and the construction of gas tight storage facilities.

For combustion and gasification of wood chips the consideration of the reference systems leads to emission- savings. Approximately 90% of the total credits originate from the reference production of heat.

ADP covers the use of fossil fuels and mineral resources; the reference substance is Antimony (Sb). For the ODP emissions of ozone-depleting gases to the air are summed up and expressed in trichlorofluoromethane (CFC 11) equivalents. As the emission sources (the use of fossil fuels) are very similar for both impact categories they are discussed together. Results are shown in Fig. 3.

With exception of the plant type AD, PI emission-sav- ings are calculated for all other biomass plant types. As mentioned in Part I of this paper, direct life cycle emissions of the plant type AD, PI are higher than for all other plant types due to the need for ignition oil in the electricity generation step. Emissions from the reference production heat are responsible for the main part of the total credits. The highest emission-savings are calculated for the plant types with the highest degree of heat utilization (AD, <50 kW and G, WC). For the plant type AD, EC the reference production of fertilizer contributes about 26% to the total credits. Depending on the composition of the substrates the reference use of organic residues contributes also a substantial part to the total credits given (e.g. 26% for the plant type AD, R).

In comparison to the UCTE electricity production mix total emissions and direct life cycle emissions are lower for all most all plant types and both impact categories. Only direct life cycle emissions from the plant type AD, PI in the impact category ODP are higher than emissions from the UCTE production mix.

direct life cycle emissions REF land

The reference system which contributes the main part to the total credits for AD plants is (depending on the share of manure/residues in the substrate mix) REF manure followed by REF residues. For combustion and gasification plants REF land contributes the main part to the total credits. Toxicity Potentials (HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP)

The impact category human toxicity potential (HTP) COV- ers the impacts of toxic substances present in the envi- ronment on human health [62]. The ecotoxicity potentials cover the impacts of toxic substances emitted to air, water and soil on terrestrial (TAETP) and aquatic (freshwater: FAETP, marine: MAETP) ecosystems. The unit of the

Fig. 3 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass-plants for the impact categories ADP, ODP and LUC considering direct life cycle emissions (positive values) and credits for reference systems (negative values)
indicator result is kg 1,4 dichlorobenzene (1,4 DCB) equivalent. Results for the different types of plants are presented in Fig. 4.
The contribution of the different reference systems to the total credits is similar for each plant type in the dif- ferent impact categories covering toxicity.
The reference production heat is important for all plant types, contributing between 22 and 98% to the total credits depending on the degree of heat utilization and the impact category.

Depending on the share of organic residues on the substrate mix the reference use of residues is important for the plant types AD, R; AD, PI and AD, <50 kW, especially for the impact categories HTP and MAETP.
For the plant type AD, EC and to a lower extend AD, <50 kW the credit for the reference production of fertilizer is responsible for a main part (up to 68%) of the total credits.

In total for most of the plant types and impact categories emissions remain even after the consideration of reference systems. Emission-savings are only calculated for the plant type G, WC in the impact category HTP and AD, R and G, WC in the impact category MAETP. The remaining

emissions are lower than the emissions caused by the UCTE electricity production mix in most of the cases under study. Only in the impact category HTP emissions from the plant type AD, <50 kW and in the impact category FAETP emissions from the plant type AD, <50 kW; C, WC and G, WC are higher than emissions from the UCTE electricity production mix.
Sensitivity analysis measures the extent to which changes in the inventory results and characterization models affect the impact indicator results.
Sensitivity Analysis: REF Manure and REF Residues

The first sensitivity analysis covers the influence of uncertainties in the calculation of gaseous emissions (and energy input) from the handling of manure and organic residues (see Tables 4, 5) on the total results for AD plants. The calculation of gaseous emissions from the handling of manure and the handling of the co-product digestate (direct

Fig. 4 Life cycle impact assessment results for 1 kWh electricity produced in different types of biomass-plants for the toxicity potentials HTP, TAETP, FAETP and MAETP considering direct life cycle emissions (positive values) and credits for reference systems (negative values)

life cycle emissions) is based on the same assumptions and calculation models (see Part I [7]). Therefore, uncertainties of total results of direct life cycle emissions caused by uncertainties from the calculation of gaseous emissions from the handling of digestate are considered in this sen- sitivity analysis. Results of this sensitivity analysis are illustrated in Fig. 5.

While direct life cycle emissions are influenced in this sensitivity analysis to the greatest extend in the impact cat- egories AP and EP (Part I [7]) the variation of total results in these impact categories is kind of compensated by the uncertainties of gaseous emissions in the "reference system

manure". For the plant types AD, M and AD, PI total results in these impact categories could shift from minimal emis- sion-savings to emissions which are then at the same level like emissions from the UCTE electricity production mix.

The highest deviation of total results is observed in the impact categories GWP, POCP and MAETP especially for plant types using a high share of organic residues in the substrate mix. This can be explained by the high uncertainty in the calculation of dinitrogen monoxide (GWP) and methane emissions (GWP and POCP) as well as energy input (MAETP) from/to the composting process (see Table 5). For the plant type AD, PI the high deviation could shift the total

Fig. 5 Sensitivity analysis: influence of uncertainties in the calculation of gaseous emissions (and energy input) from the handling of digestate, manure and organic residues on the total results for AD plants

results in the impact category POCP from being the least polluting option of all AD plants, either to being one of the most polluting options (at the level of emissions from the UCTE electricity production mix), or to being the least polluting option of all plant types. In the impact category MAETP total results could shift from emission-savings to emissions for the plant type AD, R, on the other side emis- sions could shift to emission-savings for the plant type AD, <50 kW.

The sensitivity analysis "reference production heat" shows the influence of the choice of fuel for the heat production in the reference system (100% of natural gas, coal or wood) on the total results. For the base scenario a heat supply mix consisting of 52.9% natural gas, 41.5% oil, 1.5% coal and 4.1% electricity according to [24] is assumed. The sensi- tivity analysis for the plant type G, WC, which shows the

highest degree of heat utilization and therefore, the highest alternation of total results in this sensitivity analysis is shown in Fig. 6. Results are shown in % of the base sce- nario (base scenario = 100%, white boxes) for all impact categories and 3 different heat supply fuels.

As the heat supply mix in the base scenario consists to 52.9% of natural gas, alternations of the total results in the scenario "100% natural gas" are comparable low in most of the impact categories. In general LCIA results (envi- ronmental burdens) would be higher in all impact catego- ries than in the base scenario. In the impact categories AP, POCP, TAETP and MAETP they would be nearly twice as high (in the case of POCP and MAETP shifting from emission-savings to emissions). This result can be explained by the fact that emissions from the heat supply by natural gas are lower than from the heat supply mix (base scenario).

If the fuel for the reference production heat was 100% coal, LCIA results (environmental burdens) would be reduced. Emission savings would be increased in the impact categories GWP, POCP, ADP, HTP and MAETP. Emissions would be reduced in the impact category EP. Emissions would shift to emission-savings in the impact categories AP, TAETP and FAETP. Only in the impact category ODP emission-savings would be reduced to approximately zero.

If the fuel for the reference production heat was 100% wood, the calculated emission profile would be quite dif- ferent: in the impact categories GWP, POCP, ADP, ODP and MAETP emission-savings from the base scenario would turn into low emissions. Emissions in the impact categories AP, EP, LUC and FAETP would be reduced and in the impact category TAETP shifted into emission-sav- ings. Emission-savings in the impact category HTP would be increased.

Considering direct life cycle emissions, as well as credits for various reference systems, the anaerobic digestion plant types using mainly organic residues (AD, R) shows the best environmental performance. Following

are the biomass plant type employing gasification of wood chips (G, WC), the small anaerobic digestion plant type (AD, <50 kW) and the plant type employing combustion of wood chips (C, WC). On the 5th place is the anaerobic digestion plant type using mainly manure (AD, M). At the end of the ranking the anaerobic digestion plant types using mainly energy crops (AD, EC) and using a pilot injection gas engine (AD, PI) are found.

The order of plant types is very different from the order in the ranking considering only direct life cycle emissions (see Part I [7] and Table 7). The reference systems with the strongest influence on the order of the ranking are REF heat and REF residues. The plant type AD, <50 kW, which shows the highest degree of heat utilization (of AD plants), and the plant type AD, R with a share of 69% of residues in the substrate mix, move forward 4 places in the ranking each. The plant type AD, EC which shows the lowest degree of heat utilization and no other credits for reference systems of manure or residues, falls behind 5 places.

Because a high influence of the degree of heat utilization on the total results and the order of the ranking could be identified, an optimization scenario assuming 30% heat utilization for all plant types is calculated (Table 7). This scenario allows a comparison of the different plant types without the "distortion" of different degrees of heat utili- zation. The order of plant types is again very different from

the rankings presented before. In this scenario the biomass plant type employing combustion of wood chips shows the best environmental performance, followed by AD, R; AD, M and G, WC. The plant types AD, EC; AD, <50 kW and AD, PI are found at the end of the ranking.

As the calculations are based on existing plants, the possibilities of heat utilization seem to be dependent on the location of the plant, which is characteristic for each plant type. Never the less this scenario shows the high potential of improvement of the overall environmental performance by increasing the degree of heat utilization.
68: <header>Conclusions </header>

Results for direct life cycle emissions from green elec- tricity generation in biomass plants are influenced to a great extend by the consideration of credits for the replacement of the conventional reference systems. The reference sys- items with the highest influence are the reference produc- tion of heat (POCP, ADP, ODP, toxicity potentials) and depending on the substrate mix the reference use of manure and organic residues (GWP, AP, EP, LUC). The contri- bution of the reference use of land is of minor importance. The reference production of fertilizer is only important for the anaerobic digestion plant type using mainly energy
crops, especially in the impact categories GWP and the toxicity potentials.

After the consideration of avoided burdens through the replacement of conventional reference systems emission- savings are calculated in the impact categories GWP, ADP and ODP (6 from 7 plant types), AP and EP (4 from 7 plant types). In the impact categories POCP, LUC, HTP, TAE- TP, FAETP and MAETP emissions remain for most of the biomass plant types.

In comparison with the UCTE electricity production mix the total emissions (with consideration of credits for reference systems) are lower for almost all plant types and impact categories. Only in the impact categories LUC, EP (AD, EC; C, WC; G, WC), HTP (AD, <50 kW) and FA- ETP (AD, <50 kW; C, WC; G, WC) some plant types show higher results than the UCTE electricity production mix.

The sensitivity analysis shows a high range of possible results due to uncertainties concerning the calculation of gaseous emissions from the reference systems manure and residues. Especially for AD plant types with a high share of residues in the substrate mix in the impact categories GWP, POCP and MAETP.
The choice of fuel for the reference production heat again shows a great influence on the results in all impact categories.

A ranking of the total results based on the unweighed aggregation of all impact category results shows a different picture than the ranking of direct life cycle emissions. It is lead by AD, R followed by G, WC; C, WC and AD, <50 kW. AD, M takes the 5th position and AD, EC and AD, PI are at the end of this ranking.

As potential of improvement of the overall environ- mental performance (especially in the impact categories GWP, POCP, ADP, ODP, TAETP and MAETP) an increased utilization of the co-product heat could be identified.
The detailed analysis of direct life cycle emissions in Part I [7] permitted the identification of potentials of improvement of the environmental impact of existing plants.

The present paper shows that the extension of the system boundary to include indirect environmental impacts caused by the change or replacement of the conventional provision of additional functions is essential for the overall evaluation of environmental impacts of biomass systems.

71: <header>Conclusions </header>

Results for direct life cycle emissions from green elec- tricity generation in biomass plants are influenced to a great extend by the consideration of credits for the replacement of the conventional reference systems. The reference sys- items with the highest influence are the reference produc- tion of heat (POCP, ADP, ODP, toxicity potentials) and depending on the substrate mix the reference use of manure and organic residues (GWP, AP, EP, LUC). The contri- bution of the reference use of land is of minor importance. The reference production of fertilizer is only important for the anaerobic digestion plant type using mainly energy
crops, especially in the impact categories GWP and the toxicity potentials.

After the consideration of avoided burdens through the replacement of conventional reference systems emission- savings are calculated in the impact categories GWP, ADP and ODP (6 from 7 plant types), AP and EP (4 from 7 plant types). In the impact categories POCP, LUC, HTP, TAE- TP, FAETP and MAETP emissions remain for most of the biomass plant types.

In comparison with the UCTE electricity production mix the total emissions (with consideration of credits for reference systems) are lower for almost all plant types and impact categories. Only in the impact categories LUC, EP (AD, EC; C, WC; G, WC), HTP (AD, <50 kW) and FA- ETP (AD, <50 kW; C, WC; G, WC) some plant types show higher results than the UCTE electricity production mix.

The sensitivity analysis shows a high range of possible results due to uncertainties concerning the calculation of gaseous emissions from the reference systems manure and residues. Especially for AD plant types with a high share of residues in the substrate mix in the impact categories GWP, POCP and MAETP.
The choice of fuel for the reference production heat again shows a great influence on the results in all impact categories.

A ranking of the total results based on the unweighed aggregation of all impact category results shows a different picture than the ranking of direct life cycle emissions. It is lead by AD, R followed by G, WC; C, WC and AD, <50 kW. AD, M takes the 5th position and AD, EC and AD, PI are at the end of this ranking.

As potential of improvement of the overall environ- mental performance (especially in the impact categories GWP, POCP, ADP, ODP, TAETP and MAETP) an increased utilization of the co-product heat could be identified.
The detailed analysis of direct life cycle emissions in Part I [7] permitted the identification of potentials of improvement of the environmental impact of existing plants.

The present paper shows that the extension of the system boundary to include indirect environmental impacts caused by the change or replacement of the conventional provision of additional functions is essential for the overall evaluation of environmental impacts of biomass systems.

74: <header>Conclusions </header>

Results for direct life cycle emissions from green elec- tricity generation in biomass plants are influenced to a great extend by the consideration of credits for the replacement of the conventional reference systems. The reference sys- items with the highest influence are the reference produc- tion of heat (POCP, ADP, ODP, toxicity potentials) and depending on the substrate mix the reference use of manure and organic residues (GWP, AP, EP, LUC). The contri- bution of the reference use of land is of minor importance. The reference production of fertilizer is only important for the anaerobic digestion plant type using mainly energy
crops, especially in the impact categories GWP and the toxicity potentials.

After the consideration of avoided burdens through the replacement of conventional reference systems emission- savings are calculated in the impact categories GWP, ADP and ODP (6 from 7 plant types), AP and EP (4 from 7 plant types). In the impact categories POCP, LUC, HTP, TAE- TP, FAETP and MAETP emissions remain for most of the biomass plant types.

In comparison with the UCTE electricity production mix the total emissions (with consideration of credits for reference systems) are lower for almost all plant types and impact categories. Only in the impact categories LUC, EP (AD, EC; C, WC; G, WC), HTP (AD, <50 kW) and FA- ETP (AD, <50 kW; C, WC; G, WC) some plant types show higher results than the UCTE electricity production mix.

The sensitivity analysis shows a high range of possible results due to uncertainties concerning the calculation of gaseous emissions from the reference systems manure and residues. Especially for AD plant types with a high share of residues in the substrate mix in the impact categories GWP, POCP and MAETP.
The choice of fuel for the reference production heat again shows a great influence on the results in all impact categories.

A ranking of the total results based on the unweighed aggregation of all impact category results shows a different picture than the ranking of direct life cycle emissions. It is lead by AD, R followed by G, WC; C, WC and AD, <50 kW. AD, M takes the 5th position and AD, EC and AD, PI are at the end of this ranking.

As potential of improvement of the overall environ- mental performance (especially in the impact categories GWP, POCP, ADP, ODP, TAETP and MAETP) an increased utilization of the co-product heat could be identified.
The detailed analysis of direct life cycle emissions in Part I [7] permitted the identification of potentials of improvement of the environmental impact of existing plants.

The present paper shows that the extension of the system boundary to include indirect environmental impacts caused by the change or replacement of the conventional provision of additional functions is essential for the overall evaluation of environmental impacts of biomass systems.

A ranking of the total results based on the unweighed aggregation of all impact category results shows a different picture than the ranking of direct life cycle emissions. As potential of improvement of the overall environ- mental performance (especially in the impact categories GWP, POCP, ADP, ODP, TAETP and MAETP) an increased utilization of the co-product heat could be identified.
The detailed analysis of direct life cycle emissions in Part I [7] permitted the identification of potentials of improvement of the environmental impact of existing plants.

The present paper shows that the extension of the system boundary to include indirect environmental impacts caused by the change or replacement of the conventional provision of additional functions is essential for the overall evaluation of environmental impacts of biomass systems.

77: <header>Conclusions </header>

Results for direct life cycle emissions from green elec- tricity generation in biomass plants are influenced to a great extend by the consideration of credits for the replacement of the conventional reference systems. The reference sys- items with the highest influence are the reference produc- tion of heat (POCP, ADP, ODP, toxicity potentials) and depending on the substrate mix the reference use of manure and organic residues (GWP, AP, EP, LUC). The contri- bution of the reference use of land is of minor importance. The reference production of fertilizer is only important for the anaerobic digestion plant type using mainly energy
crops, especially in the impact categories GWP and the toxicity potentials.

After the consideration of avoided burdens through the replacement of conventional reference systems emission- savings are calculated in the impact categories GWP, ADP and ODP (6 from 7 plant types), AP and EP (4 from 7 plant types). In the impact categories POCP, LUC, HTP, TAE- TP, FAETP and MAETP emissions remain for most of the biomass plant types.

In comparison with the UCTE electricity production mix the total emissions (with consideration of credits for reference systems) are lower for almost all plant types and impact categories. Only in the impact categories LUC, EP (AD, EC; C, WC; G, WC), HTP (AD, <50 kW) and FA- ETP (AD, <50 kW; C, WC; G, WC) some plant types show higher results than the UCTE electricity production mix.

The sensitivity analysis shows a high range of possible results due to uncertainties concerning the calculation of gaseous emissions from the reference systems manure and residues. Especially for AD plant types with a high share of residues in the substrate mix in the impact categories GWP, POCP and MAETP.
The choice of fuel for the reference production heat again shows a great influence on the results in all impact categories.

A ranking of the total results based on the unweighed aggregation of all impact category results shows a different picture than the ranking of direct life cycle emissions. It is lead by AD, R followed by G, WC; C, WC and AD, <50 kW. AD, M takes the 5th position and AD, EC and AD, PI are at the end of this ranking.

As potential of improvement of the overall environ- mental performance (especially in the impact categories GWP, POCP, ADP, ODP, TAETP and MAETP) an increased utilization of the co-product heat could be identified.
The detailed analysis of direct life cycle emissions in Part I [7] permitted the identification of potentials of improvement of the environmental impact of existing plants.

The present paper shows that the extension of the system boundary to include indirect environmental impacts caused by the change or replacement of the conventional provision of additional functions is essential for the overall evaluation of environmental impacts of biomass systems.
